Payments shall be reviewed annually and may rise or fall with verified income but never above the twenty-percent ceiling. Income decline shall trigger payment reduction, suspension, term extension, employment support, or disability accommodation rather than automatic loss of housing.

blocks.label: Section 7
blocks.text: Reasonable House Pricing Initiative

Every residence shall receive a Maximum Lawful Transfer Price determined primarily by habitable square footage, structural type, quality, age, condition, energy efficiency, accessibility, documented permanent improvements, regional construction cost, ordinary residential land, and public infrastructure. Market bidding, prestige, scarcity, anticipated appreciation, and expected resale profit shall not independently increase the price.

blocks.label: Section 8
blocks.text: Tiered Housing Values

Each tier shall have a base construction value, maximum lawful price per square foot, regional cost adjustment, condition and age adjustment, improvement allowance, depreciation schedule, accessibility credit, and energy-efficiency credit. The marginal lawful price per additional square foot may decline in higher tiers.

blocks.label: Section 9
blocks.text: Prohibition of Residential Resale Profit

A residence may not be sold for financial profit. The maximum resale amount equals the lawful acquisition price plus depreciated approved permanent improvements and unavoidable public assessments, less damage, deterioration, removed improvements, and depreciation, and may never exceed the current lawful transfer price. Interest, taxes, utilities, furnishings, ordinary maintenance, personal labor, and neighborhood demand do not create resale value.

blocks.label: Section 10
blocks.text: Housing Tier Advancement Credit

An owner transferring a fully paid residence to acquire a higher tier shall receive direct credit for the lawful transfer value against the replacement dwelling. Only the lawful difference remains subject to income-based payment, and prior completed ownership years shall be credited when determining the remaining term.

blocks.label: Section 11
blocks.text: National Residential Exchange

All residential transfers shall be listed through a transparent National Residential Exchange displaying lawful price, square footage, tier, condition, accessibility, energy performance, and transfer history. Where multiple qualified buyers seek one home, selection shall use a non-price method such as first complete offer, lottery, first-time-buyer priority, accessibility need, household-size compatibility, or essential-worker proximity.

blocks.label: Section 12
blocks.text: Nonprofit Rental Standard

Residential rental housing shall not be operated for private profit. Lawful providers include public housing authorities, nonprofit organizations, resident cooperatives, community land trusts, educational or medical institutions, supportive-housing providers, and individual homeowners offering a lawful accessory unit without profit.

blocks.label: Section 13
blocks.text: Lawful Rent and Small-Unit Ceiling

Rent may recover only acquisition or construction principal, maintenance, repair, utilities included, taxes, public assessments, loss protection, administration, reserve funding, accessibility, necessary staffing, and capital replacement. Studios and units not exceeding 1,200 square feet shall not exceed one dollar per square foot per month in base rent. Mandatory occupancy charges shall be included in the stated amount and may not be disguised as amenity, technology, package, parking, renewal, or administrative fees.

blocks.label: Section 14
blocks.text: Universal Income-Based Rent

Every rental shall offer income-based rent. Total mandatory housing cost shall not exceed twenty percent of the income of the highest-paid adult permanent resident. Where this amount is below actual nonprofit operating cost, the difference shall be supplied by public housing funds, community housing funds, cross-subsidy, or the Protected Human Support Trust.

blocks.label: Section 15
blocks.text: One Rental Residence

No individual may hold more than one active residential lease or equivalent occupancy right at one time, except for temporary overlap caused by relocation, education, medical care, caregiving, military assignment, separation, disaster, or comparable transition. The overlap shall ordinarily not exceed six months.

blocks.label: Section 16
blocks.text: Eviction and Foreclosure Protection

No eviction or foreclosure may occur without notice, income reassessment, hardship review, assistance screening, mediation, repayment options, and judicial authorization. A resident shall not be displaced merely to obtain a higher-paying occupant. Government error shall not produce immediate homelessness.

blocks.label: Section 17
blocks.text: Municipal Minimum Supply Duty

Every locality shall maintain sufficient Tier I and other lowest-tier ownership and rental capacity to satisfy verified local demand. A persistent waiting list, overcrowding, homelessness, displacement, or inability to place qualified residents constitutes a housing deficiency. A municipality may not satisfy its duty merely by directing residents elsewhere.

blocks.label: Section 18
blocks.text: Commercial Vacancy Conversion Trigger

When lowest-tier demand remains unmet, all substantially unused commercial property shall enter Housing Conversion Review, including vacant plazas, malls, retail stores, offices, hotels, warehouses, schools, institutional buildings, parking structures, and developed commercial land. A property is substantially unused when less than twenty-five percent of usable space supports a genuine operating business for twelve months, absent documented construction, remediation, casualty repair, probate, sale, or funded redevelopment.

blocks.label: Section 19
blocks.text: Standardized Substantial Living Areas

Converted property shall provide genuine long-term homes with private sleeping space, sanitation, potable water, sewage, heating, climate protection, electricity, ventilation, natural light or approved equivalent, fire separation, exits, food preparation, storage, acoustic privacy, internet capability, accessibility, and durable construction. Commercial conversion shall not create warehouse cubicles or permanent emergency shelters.

blocks.label: Section 20
blocks.text: Conversion Priority and Designs

The National Housing Authority shall maintain standardized designs for strip plazas, offices, warehouses, malls, hotels, schools, medical buildings, and standalone retail structures. Priority shall consider structural suitability, employment and transit access, utility capacity, safety, environmental condition, unit yield, conversion cost, schedule, and local shortage severity.

blocks.label: Section 21
blocks.text: Owner Opportunity and Compulsory Acquisition

An owner shall first be offered the opportunity to convert directly under nonprofit rules, transfer to a public or nonprofit body, join a community land trust, or sell at lawful non-speculative value. If the owner declines and verified need persists, compulsory conversion or acquisition may proceed only after notice, independent evaluation, a Housing Conversion Record, opportunity to respond, and judicial approval with lawful compensation.

blocks.label: Section 22
blocks.text: Mixed-Use Preservation

Conversion should preserve grocery stores, healthcare, childcare, local retail, repair services, small-business workspaces, transit connections, public service offices, green space, community gardens, recreation, energy systems, and other elements needed to transform abandoned commercial land into functioning neighborhoods.

blocks.label: Section 23
blocks.text: Housing Data and Review

A privacy-protected national database shall track inventory, occupancy, beneficial ownership, rental status, price, tier, condition, vacancy, construction, waiting lists, and transfer history. ONPA shall measure affordability, supply, quality, transfer time, homelessness, ownership concentration, commercial conversion, public cost, and unintended effects.

articles.summary: Housing shall serve first as a place of residence and human stability rather than an unrestricted vehicle for speculation. This Article establishes limits on residential accumulation, a guaranteed path to first ownership, controlled transfer values, nonprofit rental operation, mandatory income-based affordability, and conversion of unused commercial property when lowest-tier housing demand is unmet.

articles.title: National Rail Revitalization, Mobility Access, And Transportation Independence

blocks.text: The Commonwealth shall construct a modern national railway network capable of replacing a substantial portion of personal-vehicle commuting and short-distance air travel while connecting every state capital, major population center, regional community, and local feeder system. The system shall be affordable, convenient, domestically maintainable, technologically advanced, and usable during national emergencies.

blocks.label: Section 1
blocks.text: National Rail Authority

An independent National Rail Development and Mobility Authority shall plan corridors, build and modernize infrastructure, establish engineering and safety standards, coordinate state and local systems, certify equipment, maintain public rail data, supervise unified ticketing, manage strategic components, and coordinate domestic manufacturing.

blocks.label: Section 2
blocks.text: State Capital Hubs

Every state capital shall contain a Major State Rail Hub integrating interstate trunk rail, intrastate regional rail, commuter rail, freight where appropriate, buses, municipal transit, bicycle facilities, pedestrian access, airports, ports, and emergency transport. A hub may be distributed across coordinated stations where a single terminal would create excessive congestion or vulnerability.

blocks.label: Section 3
blocks.text: Sixteen-Line Junction Standard

Each capital hub may contain no more than sixteen principal radial corridors. The corridors shall be geographically balanced while accounting for population, terrain, existing development, employment, universities, hospitals, industry, borders, ports, and environmental constraints. Local branches, station tracks, freight sidings, and feeder services do not count toward the sixteen-line maximum.

blocks.label: Section 4
blocks.text: Junction and Feeder Network

Principal corridors shall connect to secondary junctions that distribute mobility by regional rail, commuter rail, light rail, tram, bus rapid transit, scheduled bus, rural shuttle, demand-responsive service, bicycle, and safe pedestrian routes. A locality is not served merely because a track passes nearby; practical access and timed connections are required.

blocks.label: Section 5
blocks.text: Coverage and Redundancy

Every state capital shall be directly connected to the national network. Every major metropolitan area shall receive direct service. Each state should possess at least two independent routes where geography permits, and no major region should depend permanently on one corridor.

blocks.label: Section 6
blocks.text: Frequency and Convenience

Major corridors shall provide repeated daily service, commuter service aligned to common work shifts, weekend and holiday service, coordinated transfers, night or sleeper service where justified, and reliable timetables. Low-density regions may use shorter trains and flexible feeders rather than being abandoned.

blocks.label: Section 7
blocks.text: Unified Ticketing and Offline Access

A passenger shall be able to plan and pay for multi-operator travel as one itinerary, one transaction, and one passenger record. Delay protection, transfer rebooking, refunds, accessibility information, and local transit integration shall be automatic. Smartphone ownership shall not be required; cash, physical ticketing, and offline proof of purchase shall remain available.

blocks.label: Section 8
blocks.text: Affordable Fare Standard

Rail shall operate as essential mobility infrastructure rather than a profit-maximizing monopoly. Fares shall account for distance, service type, income, trip purpose, travel frequency, age, disability, caregiving, and absence of vehicle ownership. Necessary transportation shall not consume an unreasonable share of income.

blocks.label: Section 9
blocks.text: Vehicle Non-Ownership Mobility Credit

A resident who does not own or beneficially control a personal motor vehicle may receive reduced commuter and intercity fares, free local transfers, capped monthly passes, bicycle carriage, and feeder-service credits. Temporary rental, car-sharing, employer vehicles, medical transport, and occasional borrowing shall not automatically eliminate eligibility.

blocks.label: Section 10
blocks.text: Purpose-Based Assistance

Reduced or free travel may be provided for employment, job interviews, education, training, medical care, caregiving, court appearances, government services, disability support, emergency relocation, and public service. Verification shall be simple and privacy-protective.

blocks.label: Section 11
blocks.text: Domestic Production and Open Standards

The Nation shall maintain capacity to design, manufacture, repair, and replace rail, trainsets, locomotives, passenger cars, signals, motors, brakes, power systems, switches, bridges, tunnels, maintenance equipment, software, and critical components. Systems shall use open, interoperable, repairable standards where practical and shall not depend permanently on one foreign or proprietary supplier.

blocks.label: Section 12
blocks.text: Technology Program

The Agency for the Advancement of Technology shall maintain a Rail Technology Program for high-speed rail, lightweight vehicles, regenerative braking, energy storage, automated inspection, predictive maintenance, collision prevention, resilient communication, low-noise systems, modular stations, tunnel boring, climate resilience, cybersecurity, and manual fallback operation.

blocks.label: Section 13
blocks.text: Energy and Continuity

Electrification, distributed generation, station microgrids, regenerative braking, storage, segmented power, backup energy, black-start capability, independent communications, manual dispatch, reserve crews, mobile maintenance, alternate routes, and spare components shall allow critical corridors to function during grid, internet, fuel, and supply-chain disruptions.

blocks.label: Section 14
blocks.text: Station-Centered Communities

Housing, employment, childcare, healthcare, education, groceries, public services, local business, pedestrian paths, and bicycle infrastructure shall be concentrated near stations. Rail investment shall not become a device for speculative housing profit and shall prioritize lowest-tier housing and nonprofit occupancy.

blocks.label: Section 15
blocks.text: Construction Phases

The program shall proceed through mapping and corridor preservation; connection of every state capital; regional junction expansion; high-speed and capacity construction; accessibility retrofit; and universal mobility completion. Project priority shall be based on population served, traffic reduction, access, travel time, feasibility, emergency value, geographic equity, and public benefit rather than political influence.

blocks.label: Section 16
blocks.text: Passenger Rights and Fare Continuity

Passengers possess rights to clear fares, safe and accessible travel, delay information, automatic compensation, privacy, human review of automated enforcement, and non-regression of purchased fare plans. A fare tier may not be degraded after enrollment, and higher tiers must provide genuine additional value.

blocks.label: Section 17
blocks.text: National Action Records and Review

Every major corridor, hub, high-speed project, fare policy, route discontinuation, acquisition, and construction phase requires a National Rail Action Record. ONPA shall measure ridership, cost, reliability, coverage, traffic reduction, domestic production, energy use, safety, accessibility, housing effects, delays, and cost overruns.

articles.summary: The Commonwealth shall construct a modern national railway network capable of replacing a substantial portion of personal-vehicle commuting and short-distance air travel while connecting every state capital, major population center, regional community, and local feeder system. The system shall be affordable, convenient, domestically maintainable, technologically advanced, and usable during national emergencies.

articles.title: National Education, Child Development, Productive Learning, And School Security

blocks.text: Education shall prepare every child for competent adulthood, scientific and technological participation, skilled work, civic responsibility, personal independence, creativity, physical and mental health, and continued learning. Schools shall function as secure, welcoming, full-workday institutions that support working families without reducing childhood to continuous formal instruction.

blocks.label: Section 1
blocks.text: Universal Educational Access

Public education, transportation, meals, required supplies, ordinary technology, and workday childcare shall be provided without tuition. Access shall not depend on family wealth, property, disability, geography, family structure, parent citizenship, academic performance, or ability to provide daytime supervision.

blocks.label: Section 2
blocks.text: Developmental Structure

Education shall progress through Early Childhood, Foundation, Exploratory STEM, and Advanced Preparation stages. Early stages emphasize language, numeracy, play, health, emotional regulation, and curiosity; middle stages introduce laboratory science, programming, engineering, design, data, and trades; later stages provide advanced mathematics, sciences, technical writing, career pathways, skilled trades, research, civics, and elective arts and humanities.

blocks.label: Section 3
blocks.text: STEM Priority and Arts Foundation

Science, technology, engineering, and mathematics shall be the primary academic concentration of compulsory education. Every child shall receive arithmetic, algebra, geometry, statistics, biology, chemistry, physics, earth science, programming, digital systems, engineering design, data interpretation, AI literacy, cybersecurity awareness, experimentation, and repair. Arts shall remain required as a secondary foundation through visual art, music, design, writing, performance, media, architecture, craft, and creative problem-solving.

blocks.label: Section 4
blocks.text: Higher Education Choice

At higher education, students may elect any legitimate field, including arts, humanities, social sciences, law, education, philosophy, languages, history, public administration, science, engineering, medicine, and trades. Public investment may prioritize national shortages without eliminating choice.

blocks.label: Section 5
blocks.text: Practical Competencies

Graduation shall require demonstrated competence in reading contracts, finance, taxes, employment applications, workplace communication, first aid, food preparation, digital security, personal-data protection, basic repair, transportation systems, civics, emergency preparation, conflict resolution, and evaluation of evidence and misinformation.

blocks.label: Section 6
blocks.text: Full Workday Availability

Every school shall remain available long enough to encompass a standard work shift and ordinary commuting time, generally nine to eleven supervised hours. The extended day shall combine instruction, meals, physical activity, tutoring, projects, recreation, clubs, arts, quiet study, and rest. Separate staffing structures shall prevent teachers from being required to supervise the entire extended period.

blocks.label: Section 7
blocks.text: Transportation Without Routine Drop-Off

Every student shall receive public school transportation. Routine parent drop-off and pickup shall not be the primary system. Students shall ordinarily travel by school bus, approved transit, walking, bicycle, or specialized accessible transport. Direct parent transport remains available for medical need, disability, emergency, transport failure, specialized placement, remote residence, or comparable hardship.

blocks.label: Section 8
blocks.text: Universal Workday Childcare

Every district shall provide or coordinate no-cost care for children below compulsory school age when needed during a parent or guardian’s verified work, training, education, employment search, public service, medical treatment, court attendance, commute, or emergency. Care shall include qualified staffing, age-appropriate ratios, nutrition, rest, play, development, health procedures, disability accommodation, and secure release.

blocks.label: Section 9
blocks.text: School Security Principle

Schools shall receive the highest practical security consistent with a friendly, developmentally appropriate, non-carceral environment. Security shall emphasize controlled access, early threat identification, de-escalation, mental-health intervention, emergency response, evacuation, and family reunification, without treating ordinary children as criminal suspects.

blocks.label: Section 10
blocks.text: Continuous Monitoring and Protective Staffing

School grounds and critical access points shall be monitored twenty-four hours per day through trained personnel, cameras, alarms, environmental sensors, lighting, communications, and patrols. While students are present, each campus shall maintain at least three trained protective officers, with additional staffing based on size, layout, population, and emergency-response time.

blocks.label: Section 11
blocks.text: Retired Veteran School Protection Corps

A civilian Retired Veteran School Protection Corps may recruit honorably discharged or retired veterans who pass comprehensive background, psychological, medical, child-safety, de-escalation, youth-interaction, emergency-medical, less-lethal equipment, and legal-use-of-force training. Veteran status alone is not qualification. Officers shall not enforce ordinary classroom discipline or create a militarized culture.

blocks.label: Section 12
blocks.text: Defensive Equipment and Force

Protective officers may carry nationally approved less-lethal equipment, including pepper-projectile launchers, defensive spray, shields, restraint equipment, and emergency medical gear. Such devices shall be governed as capable of serious injury. Force is permitted only to prevent imminent injury, violent entry, assault, abduction, or comparable danger and never for truancy, disrespect, passive noncompliance, ordinary disruption, punishment, or intimidation.

blocks.label: Section 13
blocks.text: Controlled Access and Badges

School buildings shall require unique credentials for students, staff, contractors, guardians, and visitors, with visitor registration, temporary badges, recorded entry and exit, and emergency override. Badges may support entry, transportation, meals, attendance, libraries, equipment, and reunification but shall not enable unrestricted behavioral tracking, commercial use, or indefinite data retention.

blocks.label: Section 14
blocks.text: Friendly Secure Design

New and renovated schools shall combine welcoming entrances, natural light, landscaped grounds, comfortable common areas, child-scaled interiors, safe outdoor recreation, and quiet spaces with controlled access, secure classroom doors, compartmentalized emergency zones, protected exits, communications, backup power, independent water and sanitation, medical response areas, and responder access.

blocks.label: Section 15
blocks.text: Mental Health and Threat Prevention

Every school shall maintain a multidisciplinary Student Safety and Support Team and access to counselors, psychologists, social workers, crisis intervention, suicide prevention, trauma care, substance-use support, bullying intervention, family referral, and emergency psychiatric care. Security personnel shall not replace mental-health professionals.

blocks.label: Section 16
blocks.text: Productive Education and Child Labor Reform

Schools may reserve a limited portion of the week for supervised, age-appropriate, low-risk productive education. The purpose is to teach reliability, punctuality, quality, cooperation, sanitation, safe tool use, inventory, planning, workplace communication, respect for labor, and the relation between effort and community benefit. Student labor shall never replace employees, reduce labor cost, fulfill private commercial contracts, or generate private profit.

blocks.label: Section 17
blocks.text: Permitted Productive Activities

Programs may include baking, school meal preparation, safe food packaging, gardening, greenhouse work, sewing and textile repair, basic woodworking, bicycle repair, electronics disassembly and supervised repair, material sorting, printing, binding, school supplies, emergency kits, library and equipment inventory, digital production, community meals, and other approved low-risk work. Each school or regional cooperative shall maintain at least one locally sourced productive program where feasible.

blocks.label: Section 18
blocks.text: Time, Rest, and Prohibited Work

Below the Advanced Preparation Stage, productive work shall ordinarily not exceed forty-five minutes in one day or two hours in one week. Older students shall ordinarily not exceed ninety minutes in one day, five hours in one week, or ten percent of total instructional time. Work must include breaks and rotation. Hazardous chemicals, heavy machinery, high voltage, dangerous heights, firearms, toxic waste, slaughter, excessive lifting, late-night work, confidential records, employee replacement, and private political or religious labor are prohibited.

blocks.label: Section 19
blocks.text: Student Benefit and Compensation

Produced value shall return to students through meals, materials, equipment, activity funds, scholarships, savings accounts, community support, or reduced school cost. Sales revenue shall enter a protected Student Productive Education Fund. Older students performing advanced or sustained productive work may receive direct compensation and educational credit.

blocks.label: Section 20
blocks.text: Opt-Out and Alternative Electives

A student or parent may opt out without explanation or penalty. The school shall provide a meaningful alternative elective in STEM, arts, music, reading, coding, research, finance, civics, language, or independent study. Parents may be responsible for special elective materials beyond standard provision, but inability to pay shall never exclude a child; the school must offer a no-cost alternative or assistance.

blocks.label: Section 21
blocks.text: Teachers, Technical Instructors, and Facilities

Teachers shall be treated as high-skill professionals. A STEM Instructor Corps may recruit engineers, scientists, programmers, clinicians, mathematicians, tradespeople, researchers, and technical retirees after instructional and child-safety training. Every secondary school shall have access to laboratories, computing, fabrication, electronics, robotics, repair spaces, technical drawing, protective equipment, and high-speed and offline resources.

blocks.label: Section 22
blocks.text: Assessment, Competency, and Pathways

Assessment shall combine examinations, laboratories, projects, oral explanation, engineering demonstrations, portfolios, team problem-solving, certifications, apprenticeships, and research. Students may advance by competency and select pathways in engineering, computing, medicine, physical sciences, mathematics, trades, manufacturing, agriculture, business, education, arts, humanities, public safety, or other fields without permanent tracking.

blocks.label: Section 23
blocks.text: Continuity and Community Use

Schools shall maintain plans for power, internet, water, transport, weather, violence, pandemic, fire, building closure, and Survival Mode. They may serve as community continuity centers for shelter, food, water, medical support, communication, childcare, charging, family reunification, and local government support.

blocks.label: Section 24
blocks.text: Funding and Review

Education shall receive priority funding sufficient for staff, transportation, childcare, meals, security, mental health, laboratories, arts, trades, special education, extended-day operation, rural access, and maintenance. ONPA shall measure literacy, mathematics, science, technical competence, safety, childcare access, family work stability, teacher retention, security outcomes, student privacy, work-program safety, and long-term quality of life.

articles.summary: Education shall prepare every child for competent adulthood, scientific and technological participation, skilled work, civic responsibility, personal independence, creativity, physical and mental health, and continued learning. Schools shall function as secure, welcoming, full-workday institutions that support working families without reducing childhood to continuous formal instruction.

articles.title: National Health Intelligence, De-Identified Medical Data, And Preventive Care

blocks.text: The Commonwealth shall aggregate de-identified medical knowledge at national scale to improve diagnosis, treatment, prevention, mental health, public-health readiness, and reduction of comorbidities without creating a named national medical surveillance system.

blocks.label: Section 1
blocks.text: National Health Intelligence Network

A distributed National Health Intelligence Network shall support diagnostic accuracy, treatment comparison, adverse-effect detection, rare-disease identification, comorbidity research, public-health planning, prevention, rehabilitation, physical health, mental health, and development of new medical methodologies.

blocks.label: Section 2
blocks.text: No Names and Minimal Identifiers

The national analytical system shall not contain names, addresses, telephone numbers, email addresses, government identifiers, financial accounts, employer or school identity, photographs, facial-recognition data, family names, or unnecessary biometrics. The most identifying ordinary fields shall be date of birth, blood type, medically relevant biological characteristics, general geographic region, and a protected nonpublic record identifier.

blocks.label: Section 3
blocks.text: Birthdate Protection

Exact birthdate may exist in a protected core when medically necessary. Researchers shall ordinarily receive only age, age band, month and year, year, or developmental stage. Exact birthdate combined with rare disease, blood type, geography, genetics, or treatment history shall be treated as potentially identifying.

blocks.label: Section 4
blocks.text: Protected Identifier and Separate Linkage

Each record shall use a cryptographically generated identifier that reveals no identity, provider, state, or government number and permits longitudinal analysis. The identity linkage shall remain outside the national analytical database with originating institutions or state custodians under separate security controls.

blocks.label: Section 5
blocks.text: State Health Data Nodes and Backups

Each state shall maintain a State Health Data Node that receives provider data, removes direct identifiers, validates quality, generates identifiers, performs state analysis, corrects errors, and synchronizes nationally. Each state shall maintain at least one physically and digitally independent backup, geographically separated, independently powered, restorable offline, and capable of operation during national disconnection.

blocks.label: Section 6
blocks.text: Distributed Architecture

The Network shall consist of state nodes, a national analytical layer, geographically distributed backups, secure research environments, isolated emergency systems, offline restoration, versioned datasets, complete logs, open technical standards, and redundant communication. No unrestricted central database shall contain every identifiable record.

blocks.label: Section 7
blocks.text: Permitted Medical Data

The Network may include diagnoses, symptoms, laboratories, imaging findings, vital signs, medication, treatment, surgery, allergy, adverse reaction, genetics under heightened protection, family risk patterns without names, mental health, substance-use treatment, disability, pregnancy, reproductive health, vaccination, rehabilitation, devices, outcomes, hospitalization, exposure, and cause of death where relevant. Collection shall remain limited to legitimate health purposes.

blocks.label: Section 8
blocks.text: Research Access

Researchers shall define the question, methodology, minimum data, security, ethics, conflicts, duration, deletion, and publication purpose. Access shall ordinarily occur inside controlled environments rather than by unrestricted download. Every access shall be logged by user, institution, data, purpose, time, export, and resulting publication.

blocks.label: Section 9
blocks.text: Prohibition of Re-Identification and Commercial Sale

No person may attempt to identify an individual from de-identified data except for a narrowly authorized immediate medical-safety purpose. Data shall not be sold, used for advertising, employment, insurance pricing, housing, credit, political targeting, ordinary law enforcement, immigration enforcement, protest monitoring, or commercial profiling.

blocks.label: Section 10
blocks.text: Mental Health and Genetic Protections

Mental-health and genetic data shall receive heightened controls. They shall not be used to restrict voting, speech, employment, housing, credit, insurance, ordinary travel, or civic status; to identify families without consent; or to support involuntary treatment without separate individualized legal process.

blocks.label: Section 11
blocks.text: Comorbidity Reduction Program

The Network shall identify combinations of physical, mental, environmental, behavioral, and social conditions that produce disability, hospitalization, premature death, or reduced quality of life. Priority areas include metabolic and cardiovascular disease, depression with chronic illness, addiction with mental illness, chronic pain with psychiatric conditions, respiratory disease with environmental exposure, dementia risk, medication interactions, and pregnancy complications.

blocks.label: Section 12
blocks.text: Diagnostic, Treatment, and Preventive Advancement

National data shall support earlier detection, differential diagnosis, rare-disease recognition, laboratory and imaging interpretation, medication-response prediction, reduction of misdiagnosis, comparison of medicines and procedures, rehabilitation outcomes, lifestyle interventions, mental-health and addiction treatment, screening, nutrition, sleep, workplace safety, maternal health, elder health, and injury prevention.

blocks.label: Section 13
blocks.text: Artificial Intelligence

AI may discover patterns and support clinical decisions only after documented data review, bias testing, clinical validation, error measurement, auditability, and human oversight. AI shall distinguish correlation from causation and may not possess final authority to deny care, confine a person, terminate benefits, or establish criminal responsibility.

blocks.label: Section 14
blocks.text: Patient Rights

Every person has the right to plain-language notice, knowledge of categories collected, correction of inaccurate data, access audit, breach notice, complaint, remedy, and equal care regardless of data status. Optional research involving identifiable contact, samples, experiments, or direct participation requires informed consent.

blocks.label: Section 15
blocks.text: Cybersecurity and Breach Response

The Network shall use encryption, segmentation, least privilege, multi-factor authentication, intrusion detection, independent penetration testing, offline backups, physical security, supply-chain review, immutable logs, incident response, and key rotation. Serious breach concealment is a constitutional offense.

blocks.label: Section 16
blocks.text: Oversight and Measurement

A National Health Data Council shall include clinicians, mental-health professionals, epidemiologists, researchers, statisticians, cybersecurity and privacy experts, patients, disability representatives, state officials, legal scholars, ethicists, and students or early-career researchers. ONPA shall measure diagnostic improvement, treatment outcomes, adverse events, comorbidities, hospitalization, research productivity, privacy breaches, re-identification attempts, public trust, cost, and unintended harm.

articles.summary: The Commonwealth shall aggregate de-identified medical knowledge at national scale to improve diagnosis, treatment, prevention, mental health, public-health readiness, and reduction of comorbidities without creating a named national medical surveillance system.

articles.title: Credit Card Regulation, Consumer Protection, And Debt Relief

blocks.text: Credit cards shall operate as limited payment and short-term borrowing tools, not as permanent systems for extracting interest from consumers unable to reduce principal. This Article prohibits youth revolving debt, unfair interest, manipulative targeting, hidden fees, and indefinite minimum-payment structures while guaranteeing a standardized interest-free repayment path.

blocks.label: Section 1
blocks.text: Minimum Age

No person under twenty-five may open, jointly hold, guarantee, or become legally liable on a consumer credit-card account. Persons under twenty-five may use debit, prepaid accounts, non-revolving secured products, or an authorized-user card carrying no personal liability. Campus solicitation and youth-directed marketing are prohibited.

blocks.label: Section 2
blocks.text: Ability-to-Repay Underwriting

Issuers shall verify individual income, existing debt, essential housing cost, court obligations, current limits, payment history, and reasonable emergency expenses. Approval may not be based primarily on predicted interest or fee revenue, and household income may not be attributed without a legal and reliable right of access.

blocks.label: Section 3
blocks.text: Interest-Rate Ceiling

The ordinary annual percentage rate shall not exceed the national short-term public lending rate plus eight percentage points and shall never exceed fifteen percent. Interest-equivalent membership, management, risk, processing, service, or required protection charges are included in the ceiling.

blocks.label: Section 4
blocks.text: Unfair Interest and Penalty Rates

Penalty interest, retroactive rate increases, interest on fees, double-cycle billing, undisclosed calculations, and interest during an approved hardship suspension are prohibited. Total interest and fees on a defined principal balance shall not exceed fifty percent of the original principal; after that threshold, all payments reduce principal.

blocks.label: Section 5
blocks.text: Minimum Payment Standard

Minimum payments shall include current lawful interest and fees plus enough principal to retire the balance within seven years if no new transactions occur. Statements shall show the current minimum, the payment and total cost for two-year and five-year payoff, and the projected payoff date.

blocks.label: Section 6
blocks.text: Twenty-Four-Month Interest-Free Benefit

Any consumer carrying a balance may activate a twenty-four-month interest-free plan by paying ten percent of the total posted balance and ceasing all use of the card. Interest and program fees stop immediately. The remaining balance is divided into equal monthly principal payments over twenty-four months, with faster payment permitted without penalty.

blocks.label: Section 7
blocks.text: Account Freeze and Recurring Charges

The issuer shall freeze the physical card, digital wallets, stored merchant credentials, convenience checks, authorized-user cards, cash advances, transfers, and replacement cards. Before enrollment, the issuer shall identify recurring charges and allow a reasonable period for migration. The issuer may not permit new use and later blame the consumer for failing to freeze the account.

blocks.label: Section 8
blocks.text: Hardship and Missed Payments

Verified unemployment, medical incapacity, disability, caregiving, disaster, deployment, or comparable hardship may extend the plan by up to twelve months without interest. One late or missed payment does not terminate protection; notice, a thirty-day cure, adjustment, and hardship review are required. Interest may never be imposed retroactively for the protected period.

blocks.label: Section 9
blocks.text: Completion and Reporting

Upon completion, the account is paid in full, no deferred interest exists, and the account closes unless the consumer separately applies for new credit. Credit reporting shall identify successful completion of an approved repayment plan rather than default, settlement, or charge-off.

blocks.label: Section 10
blocks.text: Targeted Promotion Prohibition

Issuers shall not target offers using unemployment, medical debt, housing instability, overdrafts, emergency benefits, mental-health or substance-use treatment, divorce, bereavement, gambling, payday-loan use, minimum-payment behavior, declining scores, student status, age below twenty-five, emergency searches, or other evidence of desperation or impaired judgment. Consumers meeting the same published criteria shall receive substantially equivalent terms.

blocks.label: Section 11
blocks.text: Introductory Offers, Transfers, and Rewards

Promotions shall disclose the exact expiration, ordinary rate, repayment needed within the promotional period, fees, and conditions. Deferred interest is prohibited. Payments above the minimum apply first to the highest-rate balance. Rewards may not require carrying debt, and earned rewards shall not be confiscated merely because a consumer enters hardship or repayment.

blocks.label: Section 12
blocks.text: Fees and Data

Inactivity, account closure, repayment enrollment, payment-by-phone, online payment, statement access, limit increase, declined transaction, and duplicate late fees are prohibited. Purchase history shall not be sold or used to target harmful financial products. Medical, political, religious, location, or behavioral data shall not determine price.

blocks.label: Section 13
blocks.text: Collection and Debt Sale

Collectors may not threaten arrest, disclose debt, humiliate, contact excessively, misstate amounts, or pressure medically incapacitated consumers without accommodation. A debt buyer acquires no greater rights than the issuer and must possess complete agreements, payment, interest, fee, dispute, and ownership documentation. Undocumented debt is unenforceable.

blocks.label: Section 14
blocks.text: National Awareness Campaign

A permanent, publicly funded, industry-independent campaign shall explain revolving debt, compounding, minimum-payment traps, fees, promotions, balance transfers, credit scores, collection, fraud, alternatives, and the twenty-four-month benefit. Before receiving a first card at age twenty-five, an applicant shall complete a free standardized education module. Secondary schools shall teach practical debt calculations without credit-industry sponsorship.

blocks.label: Section 15
blocks.text: Enforcement and Review

Remedies include cancellation of unlawful interest and fees, balance recalculation, refunds, damages, interest-free repayment, reporting correction, suspension of account issuance, promotion restrictions, executive clawbacks, probation, blacklisting, revocation of issuing authority, and prosecution for fraud. ONPA shall measure debt, interest, fees, payoff time, bankruptcy, education, plan use, youth exposure, promotion violations, availability of responsible credit, and movement toward unregulated lending.

articles.summary: Credit cards shall operate as limited payment and short-term borrowing tools, not as permanent systems for extracting interest from consumers unable to reduce principal. This Article prohibits youth revolving debt, unfair interest, manipulative targeting, hidden fees, and indefinite minimum-payment structures while guaranteeing a standardized interest-free repayment path.

articles.title: National Circular Sanitation, Water Conservation, And Elimination Of Conventional Water-Carried Sewage

blocks.text: Clean water is a strategic national resource. The routine use of potable or reusable water merely to transport human waste shall be progressively eliminated. The Nation shall replace the flush, dilute, transport, treat, and discharge model with circular sanitation based on separation, concentration, pathogen destruction, recovery, reuse, resilience, and minimal residual waste.

blocks.label: Section 1
blocks.text: National Circular Sanitation Authority

An independent National Circular Sanitation Authority shall establish technical and public-health standards, coordinate states and municipalities, fund modernization, approve technology, maintain sanitation data, certify facilities, establish contaminant limits, coordinate manufacturing, supervise pilots, enforce milestones, and ensure service continuity.

blocks.label: Section 2
blocks.text: Public Health First

No conservation, decentralization, recovery, cost savings, or experiment shall justify exposure to untreated human waste, contaminated drinking water or food, uncontrolled pathogens, unsafe indoor conditions, or denial of sanitation. Existing systems shall remain maintained until safe replacements are constructed, tested, staffed, supplied, and certified.

blocks.label: Section 3
blocks.text: Waste-Stream Separation

Human fecal matter, urine, greywater, stormwater, food waste, industrial wastewater, hospital and laboratory wastewater, pharmaceutical and chemical wastewater, agricultural runoff, petroleum-contaminated water, hazardous biological waste, and other specialized streams shall be separated wherever mixing causes dilution, contamination, treatment difficulty, resource loss, or avoidable energy use.

blocks.label: Section 4
blocks.text: New Construction and Major Renovation

New construction shall progressively adopt approved waterless, vacuum, ultra-low-water, urine-diverting, source-separating, composting, sealed collection, biological treatment, digestion, or equivalent systems. Major renovations shall add separate greywater, heat recovery, source-separation readiness, reduced-water fixtures, maintenance access, backflow protection, and neighborhood connections where reasonably practical.

blocks.label: Section 5
blocks.text: Water Quality Classes and Lowest Safe Use

National standards shall distinguish potable, sanitary, utility, agricultural, industrial-process, ecological-restoration, and other water classes. New buildings and infrastructure shall use the lowest safe quality for each purpose. Potable water shall be reserved primarily for drinking, food preparation, medicine, and direct human consumption.

blocks.label: Section 6
blocks.text: Greywater Recovery

Greywater shall be separated and treated locally or regionally for irrigation, cleaning, sanitation, cooling, industrial processes, fire protection, groundwater recharge, ecological restoration, and other non-potable purposes. Potable reuse requires full drinking-water standards and verified cross-connection protection.

blocks.label: Section 7
blocks.text: Human Waste as Resource

Human excreta and compatible organic material shall be treated as recoverable biological resources. Systems shall recover phosphorus, nitrogen, organic carbon, biogas, heat, reusable water, soil-building material, industrial feedstock, and other useful outputs where safe. Recovery never supersedes pathogen destruction or contaminant control.

blocks.label: Section 8
blocks.text: Resource Recovery and Sanitation Facilities

Conventional treatment plants shall be converted or replaced by Resource Recovery and Sanitation Facilities evaluated by pathogen destruction, contaminant removal, water recovery, nutrient recovery, energy balance, heat recovery, residual generation, reliability, cost, and environmental restoration rather than by discharge compliance alone.

blocks.label: Section 9
blocks.text: Distributed and Centralized Balance

Building, neighborhood, municipal, regional, and centralized systems shall be selected according to density, geography, health, cost, workforce, transport distance, resilience, maintenance, and growth. Communities shall not be forced into enormous sewer networks where smaller systems are safer and more resilient, nor prohibited from centralized facilities where density and complexity justify them.

blocks.label: Section 10
blocks.text: Industrial Pretreatment and Contaminant Responsibility

Industrial, hospital, laboratory, pharmaceutical, chemical, and specialized facilities shall separate and treat hazardous or persistent streams before public discharge. Producers introducing PFAS, pharmaceuticals, endocrine disruptors, microplastics, nanomaterials, heavy metals, solvents, antimicrobials, and comparable contaminants shall finance detection, separation, recovery, neutralization, destruction, or secure containment after use.

blocks.label: Section 11
blocks.text: Agricultural and Commercial Reuse

No sanitation-derived nutrient, organic material, fertilizer, soil amendment, water, or commercial product may be used where pathogens, pharmaceuticals, PFAS, metals, microplastics, hormones, industrial chemicals, or other dangerous contaminants exceed strict standards accounting for long-term accumulation, groundwater, food-chain transfer, workers, and ecology.

blocks.label: Section 12
blocks.text: Heat and Energy Recovery

New large buildings, hospitals, laundries, districts, industrial sites, and sanitation facilities shall evaluate wastewater heat recovery. Facilities shall evaluate digestion, biogas, thermal recovery, and energy storage using complete lifecycle accounting that includes transport, chemicals, maintenance, and residual treatment.

blocks.label: Section 13
blocks.text: Emergency Continuity

Sanitation shall operate during grid, internet, fuel, natural-disaster, war, cyberattack, supply-chain, regional-isolation, and Survival Mode conditions. Critical systems shall maintain backup power, gravity or passive modes, emergency storage, offline controls, manual procedures, local authentication, reserve chemicals, portable treatment, mobile toilets, replacement pumps and valves, emergency crews, and independent communication.

blocks.label: Section 14
blocks.text: Domestic Manufacturing and Open Standards

The Nation shall manufacture or rapidly replace pumps, vacuum systems, pipes, valves, tanks, membranes, sensors, digesters, separators, heat exchangers, treatment equipment, chemicals, controls, and mobile sanitation equipment. Systems shall be modular, repairable, interchangeable, documented, supported for long lives, and operable offline. Remote disabling over licensing or contract disputes is prohibited.

blocks.label: Section 15
blocks.text: Technology Challenges and Pilot Communities

The Agency for the Advancement of Technology shall run performance competitions for waterless sanitation, vacuum systems, source separation, pathogen destruction, nutrient recovery, PFAS destruction, pharmaceutical removal, microplastic capture, compact treatment, greywater, heat, emergency sanitation, rural systems, accessibility, odor, and user experience. Pilot communities require informed public participation, health and environmental monitoring, backup service, independent review, transparent records, complaint rights, and termination criteria.

blocks.label: Section 16
blocks.text: Phased Fifty-Year Transition

The transition shall not exceed fifty years and shall proceed through national measurement and preparation; conversion of new construction; municipal and industrial modernization; existing-building retrofit during major renovation; retirement of obsolete sewer districts; and final prohibition of routine potable-water waste transport and discharge of recoverable sanitation resources where approved alternatives are reasonably available.

blocks.label: Section 17
blocks.text: Existing Systems and Combined Overflows

Existing sewers shall be repaired throughout transition. Potable use, leakage, combined sewer overflows, stormwater mixing, industrial contamination, and untreated discharge shall be progressively eliminated while recovery increases. Repeated overflow without correction shall trigger intervention; rainfall is not a permanent justification for sewage discharge.

blocks.label: Section 18
blocks.text: Materials Database and Privacy

A National Sanitation Materials Database shall track aggregate generation, transport, treatment, water, nutrients, energy, materials, destroyed contaminants, residuals, and discharge. Sanitation data shall not identify personal medical conditions, drug use, pregnancy, genetics, reproduction, diet, politics, religion, or relationships except for a narrowly defined immediate public-health emergency under independent legal oversight.

blocks.label: Section 19
blocks.text: Ownership and Affordability

Essential sanitation shall be publicly, cooperatively, or nonprofit owned, or operated as a tightly regulated utility. Revenue shall support operations, maintenance, workforce, modernization, reserves, health, emergency readiness, and replacement rather than excessive profit. No household shall lose safe sanitation because of inability to pay.

blocks.label: Section 20
blocks.text: Enforcement and Review

Corrective orders, mandatory repair and pretreatment, discharge suspension, resident compensation, environmental restoration, penalties, contaminant payments, executive clawbacks, operational control, probation, blacklisting, license revocation, and criminal prosecution may address contamination, concealment, dilution to evade limits, misclassification, falsification, monitoring interference, unsafe export, and unauthorized discharge. Every major program requires a concise National Action Record and ONPA measurement.

articles.summary: Clean water is a strategic national resource. The routine use of potable or reusable water merely to transport human waste shall be progressively eliminated. The Nation shall replace the flush, dilute, transport, treat, and discharge model with circular sanitation based on separation, concentration, pathogen destruction, recovery, reuse, resilience, and minimal residual waste.

articles.title: Consumer Services, Continuing Payments, Digital Property, And Service Continuity

blocks.text: Consumer services shall remain profitable because consumers continue to choose them and providers continue to deliver promised value. No business shall obtain continuing revenue primarily through confusion, artificial dependency, involuntary renewal, service regression, hidden charges, revocable digital ownership, or destruction of purchased value.

blocks.label: Section 1
blocks.text: Scope and Exclusions

This Article governs subscriptions, memberships, automatic renewals, digital platforms, software and cloud services, streaming, telecommunications, internet access, gaming, data storage, AI services, monitoring, maintenance plans, repair plans, support programs, delivery memberships, fitness and recreation memberships, education services, transportation memberships, connected-vehicle services, recurring deliveries, and similar continuing or access-based consumer arrangements. It does not govern rent, mortgages, lending, credit agreements, insurance, taxes, government fees, court-ordered payments, or ordinary installment purchases resulting in ownership without a continuing service.

blocks.label: Section 2
blocks.text: Consumer Service Record and Dashboard

Every covered service shall maintain a standardized record stating provider, service, price, billing frequency, enrollment, renewal, benefits, limitations, advertising, data practices, cancellation, refunds, change history, and consent history. An interoperable national dashboard shall allow consumers to view active services, monthly and annualized cost, billing and renewal dates, trials, changes, and cancellation methods without permitting government sale or commercial profiling of preferences.

blocks.label: Section 3
blocks.text: Price Continuity and Non-Regression

Once a consumer enters at an agreed recurring price, the provider may not increase that price while providing a materially equivalent service. Once at least one person subscribes to a defined tier, the tier may not regress for existing subscribers. Prohibited regression includes adding advertisements to an ad-free service, increasing ad intrusion, removing features, reducing quality, storage, usage, performance, devices, interoperability, privacy, support, or access, converting included benefits into paid add-ons, or manufacturing restrictions to force an upgrade.

blocks.label: Section 4
blocks.text: Genuine Higher Tiers

A higher-priced plan must add substantial products, services, content categories, functionality, productive capacity, usage, physical service, or professional capability. Restoring removed features, removing newly added ads, ordinary bug or security fixes, ordinary quality, customer support necessary for use, renaming, repackaging, and artificial separation of integrated features do not constitute added value.

blocks.label: Section 5
blocks.text: Renewal, Trials, Cancellation, and Dormant Services

Automatic renewal requires affirmative enrollment and clear notice before substantial, annual, discounted-to-full-price, or free-to-paid renewal. Cancellation shall be no harder than enrollment, available through the same medium, immediate in effect for future billing, and free of retention interviews, hidden pages, unnecessary calls, certified mail, delay, or explanation requirements. Dormant services shall trigger a notice showing inactivity, amount paid, next charge, and direct cancellation; prolonged unused service may require renewed consent.

blocks.label: Section 6
blocks.text: Bundles, Embedded Features, and Dependency

A provider may not force unrelated bundles, remove individually available service to compel a bundle, hide component prices, or move existing benefits into a higher-cost package. A physical capability already included in a purchased product may not require recurring payment unless its operation creates a genuine ongoing provider cost clearly disclosed before purchase. Purchased physical products shall remain reasonably functional without unrelated cloud services.

blocks.label: Section 7
blocks.text: Digital Property Purchase

A one-time payment for software, games, applications, downloadable content, films, television, music, audiobooks, electronic books, publications, art, educational materials, tools, upgrades, or other digital goods creates a permanent property interest when a reasonable consumer expects indefinite personal use. Labeling the transaction a license, entitlement, activation, or permission does not defeat its substance.

blocks.label: Section 8
blocks.text: No Revocation of Digital Licenses

A provider shall not revoke, expire, disable, confiscate, or materially impair lawfully purchased digital property after payment, except for fraud, lawful payment reversal, final judicial determination that possession is unlawful, voluntary transfer, or a court remedy directly related to misuse of that property. Unrelated account violations shall not confiscate separate purchases, and account suspension shall not eliminate access to owned digital property.

blocks.label: Section 9
blocks.text: Offline Access and Local Operation

Digital property shall support offline installation, local authentication, local storage, backup, restoration, and continued use during network or provider failure wherever technically practical. Continuous internet connection may not be required solely for advertising, surveillance, behavioral collection, resale prevention, or commercial control. Products inherently dependent on live interaction, remote computation, streaming, social networking, marketplaces, or continuously updated data may remain online-dependent if disclosed clearly before payment.

blocks.label: Section 10
blocks.text: Online Service Continuity Plan

Any online-dependent product accepting consumer payments shall maintain a public continuity plan describing expected duration, infrastructure dependencies, planned and unexpected shutdown, notice, data export, refund, credit, preservation, and transfer. A provider shall not accept substantial payment while knowingly planning imminent termination without prominent disclosure.

blocks.label: Section 11
blocks.text: Planned and Unexpected Shutdown

Planned permanent shutdown shall ordinarily provide twelve months notice and pursue offline conversion, release of server software, community servers, transfer to another provider, interoperability, removal of authentication, technical documentation, preservation of purchases, refunds, or credits. Unexpected shutdown due to bankruptcy, acquisition, infrastructure failure, supplier loss, dissolution, or government action gives consumers a priority claim for unused or destroyed value against the provider, successor, acquirer, or bankruptcy estate.

blocks.label: Section 12
blocks.text: Compensation for Lost Online Value

Prepaid and fixed-term service shall be refunded for the unused period. Recently purchased online-dependent products and persistent digital purchases shall receive compensation based on price, time since purchase, expected useful duration, actual availability, remaining value, promised permanence, and ability to transfer. Compensation may be money, proportional refund, equal or greater credit, successor access, or permanent offline access, but credit shall not require substantial additional spending.

blocks.label: Section 13
blocks.text: Shutdown Reserves and Successor Liability

Large providers may be required to maintain reserves, escrow, continuity trusts, or equivalent security proportionate to consumers, prepaid value, persistent purchases, financial stability, and shutdown cost. Acquirers, successor entities, asset buyers, and reorganized companies acquire continuity and non-regression obligations. A merger or renaming cannot erase consumer rights.

blocks.label: Section 14
blocks.text: Preservation, Repair, Transfer, and Inheritance

Abandoned products should be preserved through removal of authentication, release of executables, server software, source code where safe, archival transfer, technical documentation, offline modes, community operation, or a National Digital Preservation Repository. Owners may develop compatibility tools, replacement servers, emulators, and repair software for continued lawful use. Digital property shall be transferable, giftable, inheritable, and resalable where technically practical, with anti-duplication controls allowed.

blocks.label: Section 15
blocks.text: Algorithmic Manipulation and Enforcement

Providers shall not use behavioral data or AI to create cancellation obstacles, conceal lower-cost plans, selectively increase price based on dependency, or exploit psychological vulnerability. Remedies include restoration, baseline enforcement, refunds, credits, compensation, statutory damages, mandatory offline functionality, preservation release, executive clawbacks, monitoring, probation, blacklisting, and charter revocation for persistent abuse.

articles.summary: Consumer services shall remain profitable because consumers continue to choose them and providers continue to deliver promised value. No business shall obtain continuing revenue primarily through confusion, artificial dependency, involuntary renewal, service regression, hidden charges, revocable digital ownership, or destruction of purchased value.

articles.title: Public Corrections, Swift Adjudication, Proportional Sentencing, And Permanent Deprivatization Of Incarceration

blocks.label: Section 1
blocks.text: Purpose and Constitutional Principle

The criminal justice and corrections systems shall protect the public, determine guilt reliably, prevent repeated harm, rehabilitate persons capable of safe return, and permanently separate persons whose proven conduct establishes an extraordinary and continuing danger. No person or institution shall profit from incarceration or from increasing arrests, convictions, sentence lengths, detention populations, or denials of release.

Punishment shall be imposed only after due process and shall correspond to actual harm, intent, continuing danger, recurrence, and capacity for rehabilitation. The rights to counsel, silence, confrontation, an impartial tribunal, proof beyond a reasonable doubt, appeal, and review of newly discovered evidence remain preserved.

blocks.label: Section 2
blocks.text: Permanent Deprivatization of Incarceration

All prisons, jails, detention centers, correctional compounds, secure treatment facilities, and juvenile facilities exercising involuntary criminal confinement shall be publicly owned and publicly controlled.

items: No private organization may own or operate a correctional institution.
No contract may reward occupancy, sentence duration, denial of parole, or increased detention.

Private specialists may provide defined medical, educational, construction, food, or maintenance services only under transparent fixed-price public-service contracts unrelated to incarceration levels.
The government remains legally responsible for every confined person and may not transfer constitutional liability to a contractor.

blocks.label: Section 3
blocks.text: National Corrections Standards Authority

An independent National Corrections Standards Authority shall establish uniform standards for classification, detention, education, healthcare, sanitation, nutrition, staffing, discipline, inspection, release preparation, and public reporting. The Authority shall remain independent from prosecutors, police, prison administrators, correctional unions in matters of inspection, and private contractors.

blocks.label: Section 4
blocks.text: National Sentencing Classifications
Offenses shall be classified by actual harm, intent, coercion, violence, victim vulnerability, recurrence, and rehabilitation potential.

rows: Little or no direct physical harm and limited continuing danger
Structured education, restitution, supervision, or reintegration-compound detention where necessary
Substantial financial, environmental, institutional, psychological, or societal harm without intentional physical violence
Mandatory adult education, restitution, occupational restrictions, and additional jury-authorized measures
Assault, battery, arson endangering persons, and comparable intentional violence
Secure confinement beginning at three years and increasing with severity
Intentional murder, rape, kidnapping, sexual offenses against children, and constitutionally comparable extraordinary offenses
Lifelong secure individual separation after the highest level of adjudicative safeguards

blocks.label: Section 5
blocks.text: Swift Adjudication Without Summary Punishment

Cases supported by clear, substantial, lawfully obtained, independently reviewable, and materially uncontested evidence shall proceed without unnecessary delay. Swift sentencing shall never mean conviction by accusation or administrative declaration.

items: The accused shall receive complete material evidence and qualified counsel.
Forensic evidence shall receive independent review where material.
Confessions shall be examined for coercion, incapacity, and unreliability.
The accused retains the right to contest guilt and demand ordinary trial.
Accelerated procedure requires approval by a neutral judge and preserves appeal for substantial legal error.

blocks.label: Section 6
blocks.text: Pretrial Detention

Pretrial detention is not punishment and shall occur only when specific evidence establishes a serious risk of flight, witness or evidence interference, immediate violence, or repeated serious crime that cannot reasonably be controlled through less restrictive means. Poverty shall not determine confinement, and cash bail that detains solely for inability to pay is prohibited.

blocks.label: Section 7
blocks.text: Low-Harm Offenses and Six-Month Reintegration Detention

Offenses causing little or no actual harm to another person shall ordinarily receive education, restitution, supervision, and structured correction rather than conventional imprisonment. Where confinement is necessary, the standard minimum shall be six months in a National Reintegration Compound, subject to judicial reduction where confinement would produce no measurable benefit.

blocks.label: Section 8
blocks.text: National Reintegration Compounds

Reintegration Compounds shall use a secure barracks-style structure with strict but humane schedules. Their purpose is discipline, education, lawful work preparation, civic understanding, and return to independent society rather than isolation or profit.

items: Daily schedules shall balance education, physical activity, facility responsibility, meals, counseling where appropriate, rest, and release preparation.
Compounds shall not be designed to create psychological deprivation or uncontrolled violence.
Residents shall receive basic needs, qualified instruction, and measurable competency standards.

blocks.label: Section 9
blocks.text: Mandatory Adult Education
Every person sentenced for a nonviolent offense shall receive an individual assessment and a Correction and Education Plan. Completion requires demonstrated competence rather than attendance alone.

items: Literacy, mathematics, financial and digital literacy
Employment skills, workplace conduct, and technical certification
Civic knowledge, constitutional rights, and legal understanding
Conflict management, decision-making, and consequence analysis
Health, nutrition, parenting, substance-use education, and other needs materially connected to lawful independence

blocks.label: Section 10
blocks.text: Serious Nonviolent Offenses and Sentencing Juries

Major fraud, corruption, large-scale theft, severe environmental contamination, systemic cybercrime, financial exploitation, and other substantial nonviolent harms shall remain centered on education and restitution. A sentencing jury may authorize measures beyond education where the harm, intent, concealment, recurrence, or continuing risk makes them necessary.

items: Extended structured detention
Long-term supervised release
Occupational or fiduciary restrictions directly related to the offense
Financial restrictions and restitution
Mandatory public service or additional education
Other proportionate measures consistent with constitutional rights

blocks.label: Section 11
blocks.text: Restitution and Financial Accountability

Restitution to identifiable victims shall receive priority over revenue-producing fines. A capable offender may contribute a reasonable portion of earnings, but restitution shall not prevent minimum housing, food, healthcare, dependent support, or lawful reintegration.

blocks.label: Section 12
blocks.text: Correctional Labor

Correctional labor shall never be supplied to private organizations as below-market labor. Work must serve education, skill development, facility operation, public infrastructure, environmental restoration, production of public goods, restitution, or community benefit. Substantial productive work shall receive fair institutional compensation allocated among restitution, dependent support, personal savings, and transition funds.

blocks.label: Section 13
blocks.text: Violent Offenses

Intentional assault, battery, arson creating substantial danger to persons, and comparable violent offenses shall require secure confinement for not less than three years. The sentence shall increase with injury, planning, weapons, victim vulnerability, lasting disability, number of victims, and demonstrated danger.

blocks.label: Section 14
blocks.text: Secure Institutions for Violent Offenders

Persons convicted of violent offenses shall be separated from education-centered reintegration compounds. Security classification shall reflect actual institutional risk. Education, psychological evaluation, treatment, and release preparation shall remain available where eventual release is lawful.

blocks.label: Section 15
blocks.text: Repeat Violent Offenses and Indefinite Confinement

A person who completes a sentence for a Classification III offense and later commits another intentional offense involving assault, battery, arson endangering persons, or comparable serious violence may be sentenced to indefinite secure confinement. The person may request a parole hearing once every three years.

blocks.label: Section 16
blocks.text: Parole Review and Sequential Denial

Parole review shall examine continuing danger, institutional conduct, education, responsibility, psychological evaluation, release planning, victim impact, and sustained behavioral change. After three sequential denials occurring at least three years apart, ordinary parole eligibility may terminate only after an independent judicial panel confirms that each hearing was fair and supported by substantial evidence. Extraordinary review remains available for innocence, new evidence, or constitutional error.

blocks.label: Section 17
blocks.text: Permanent Separation Offenses

Intentional murder, rape, kidnapping, sexual offenses against children, and other offenses expressly designated by constitutional law as permanent-separation offenses shall be punished by lifelong incarceration without ordinary release.

items: Unanimous jury conviction
Automatic independent appellate review
Independent forensic review and disclosure of all exculpatory evidence
Review for false confession, coercion, mistaken identification, forensic error, prosecutorial misconduct, and ineffective counsel
Permanent preservation of biological and material evidence capable of later testing

blocks.label: Section 18
blocks.text: Permanent Individual Separation

A person lawfully sentenced for a permanent-separation offense shall remain in individual secure housing, separated from the general population and from direct social interaction with other incarcerated persons. Routine delivery, observation, and administration shall occur through secure non-contact systems where practical.

Permanent separation shall not authorize torture, deliberate starvation, sleep deprivation, extreme temperature, denial of sanitation, denial of medical care, sensory assault, or intentional disease exposure. Direct human contact shall be limited to medical care, emergency response, legal process, constitutional inspection, essential maintenance, and necessary administration.

blocks.label: Section 19
blocks.text: Basic Sustenance Standard

Permanent Separation Units may provide nutritionally complete standardized meals, protein supplementation, vitamins, minerals, and safe water sufficient to preserve life and physical health. Food need not provide luxury or entertainment, but shall not create nutritional deficiency. Independent medical personnel shall monitor sufficiency.

blocks.label: Section 20
blocks.text: Conditions and Security of Permanent Separation Units

items: Safe drinking water and sanitation
Basic sleeping accommodation and temperature control sufficient for health
Basic exercise sufficient to prevent avoidable physical deterioration
Medical monitoring and emergency communication
Access to legal proceedings and independent inspection
Continuous security designed to prevent escape, assault, and self-harm

blocks.label: Section 21
blocks.text: Prohibition of Punishment as Spectacle

Permanent confinement exists to protect society through separation, not to create public entertainment. Government shall not sell access, broadcast confinement for entertainment, commercially exploit notoriety, or stage punishment for humiliation.

blocks.label: Section 22
blocks.text: Persons Under Eighteen

No person who was under eighteen at the time of an offense shall automatically receive permanent separation. For kidnapping or a sexual offense against a child, a specialized sentencing jury shall examine age, maturity, planning, coercion, mental capacity, prior victimization, harm, predatory intent, recurrence, rehabilitation potential, and continuing danger.

blocks.label: Section 23
blocks.text: Ten-Year Mandatory Education for Serious Juvenile Offenses

Where the jury finds permanent separation unjustified but determines that the under-eighteen offense was serious, it may impose not less than ten years of intensive education and rehabilitation. Completion of ten years does not itself compel release; release requires a finding that unreasonable danger no longer exists.

items: Compulsory academic and technical education
Psychological and behavioral treatment
Victim-awareness and impulse-control education
Specialized sexual-behavior treatment where applicable
Supervised social development, progressive responsibility, and continuing risk assessment

blocks.label: Section 24
blocks.text: Juvenile Review

Serious juvenile sentences shall receive comprehensive review at least every two years to measure education, maturity, treatment, conduct, danger, responsibility, and capacity for lawful independence.

blocks.label: Section 25
blocks.text: Wrongful Conviction Safeguards

An independent National Conviction Integrity Office shall investigate credible claims of innocence and possess access to preserved forensic evidence, prosecution and police files, laboratory records, interrogation recordings, witness statements, and correctional records. Obstruction, concealment, destruction, or falsification of material evidence is a serious offense.

blocks.label: Section 26
blocks.text: Compensation for Wrongful Confinement

A factually innocent person wrongfully confined shall receive compensation addressing duration, lost income, lost property, educational interruption, physical and psychological harm, family disruption, medical needs, housing, and reintegration. Malice by an individual official need not be proven where the conviction itself is overturned for factual innocence.

blocks.label: Section 27
blocks.text: Medical and Mental Health Care

Government assumes responsibility for medically necessary care of every confined person. Correctional healthcare shall not reward denial of treatment. Persons incompetent to understand proceedings shall not undergo ordinary adjudication until competency standards are met. Secure public treatment institutions shall be used where severe mental disorder or cognitive impairment makes ordinary prison clinically inappropriate.

blocks.label: Section 28
blocks.text: Correctional Discipline and Use of Force

Institutional discipline shall use written rules, notice, evidence, opportunity to respond, neutral review, written findings, and appeal for serious sanctions. Force may be used only for protection, control, escape prevention, or immediate security and shall never be used as informal punishment.

blocks.label: Section 29
blocks.text: Correctional Staff

Correctional employment shall be a professional public-security occupation with training in constitutional rights, de-escalation, emergency medicine, mental-health recognition, evidence preservation, force, fire response, suicide prevention, corruption prevention, and ethics. Staffing and compensation shall be sufficient to reduce neglect, violence, turnover, and corruption.

blocks.label: Section 30
