blocks.text: Inspection and Public Conditions Database

Every facility shall undergo announced and unannounced inspection. The National Correctional Conditions Database shall publish facility-level population, capacity, safety, deaths, suicides, force, education, costs, staffing, medical complaints, abuse findings, inspection results, and corrective orders while protecting private medical information.

blocks.label: Section 31
blocks.text: Prohibition of Financial Incentives for Confinement

No budget, compensation system, contract, or performance measure shall reward increased incarceration. Success shall be measured by public safety, conviction accuracy, lawful adjudication speed, education, reintegration, restitution, facility safety, prevention of abuse, and cost efficiency consistent with rights.

blocks.label: Section 32
blocks.text: Release Preparation and Supervision

Persons eligible for release shall receive identification, housing planning, employment access, education, healthcare, transportation, financial access, family coordination, and continuing treatment. Supervision conditions must relate to demonstrated risk, and technical violations causing no new harm shall not automatically produce lengthy reincarceration.

blocks.label: Section 33
blocks.text: Victim Rights

items: Timely information and protection from intimidation
Opportunity to submit impact evidence and seek restitution
Notice and participation in release proceedings as provided by law
Privacy protection and access to public support services
Review of substantial correctional failures affecting safety

blocks.label: Section 34
blocks.text: National Action Records and Independent Measurement

Every major sentencing policy, facility project, educational program, parole standard, security mandate, and correctional reform shall possess a National Action Record. ONPA shall measure crime recurrence, conviction accuracy, adjudication time, pretrial detention, education, employment, housing, restitution, institutional violence, deaths, suicide, medical outcomes, cost, wrongful convictions, parole, and public safety after release.

blocks.label: Section 35
blocks.text: Enforcement, Complaints, and Judicial Review

Serious correctional misconduct shall receive external investigation and may result in removal, permanent disqualification, civil liability, restitution, license loss, criminal prosecution, contractor blacklisting, or institutional receivership. Confined persons, families, staff, attorneys, clinicians, inspectors, and citizens may submit confidential complaints without retaliation. Courts retain authority over sentences, conditions, parole, discipline, innocence claims, and constitutional violations.

blocks.label: Section 36
blocks.text: Transition and Final Standard

Private correctional contracts shall end, facilities shall transfer to public control or close, and every confined person shall receive classification review. Low-harm and nonviolent offenders shall be evaluated for education-centered placement; violent offenders shall enter appropriate secure public institutions.

The system shall educate where education can correct, confine where confinement is necessary, permanently separate only after the highest standard of proof and review, and never permit captivity to become a commercial industry.

articles.summary: The criminal justice and corrections systems shall protect the public, determine guilt reliably, prevent repeated harm, rehabilitate persons capable of safe return, and permanently separate persons whose proven conduct establishes an extraordinary and continuing danger. No person or institution shall profit from incarceration or from increasing arrests, convictions, sentence lengths, detention populations, or denials of release.

articles.title: Passenger Vehicle Efficiency, Commuter Mobility, And Right-Sized Transportation

blocks.label: Section 1
blocks.text: National Purpose

Passenger transportation shall preserve personal mobility while reducing unnecessary consumption of energy, road capacity, materials, land, parking, and household income. The routine transportation of one person in a vehicle substantially larger and heavier than necessary imposes avoidable congestion, road wear, collision energy, tire and brake pollution, and infrastructure cost.
The objective is not to prohibit large vehicles. It is to make the smallest safe and practical transportation option the most affordable and convenient choice for routine single-person travel.

blocks.label: Section 2
blocks.text: Right to Personal Mobility

Every person retains the right to lawful private transportation and shall not be forced to use public transit. Regulation shall use voluntary incentives, infrastructure design, safety standards, and measured congestion policy rather than requiring government permission for ordinary travel. Disability, family, occupational, rural, emergency, and terrain needs shall receive accommodation.

blocks.label: Section 3
blocks.text: Transportation According to Actual Need

Policy shall distinguish vehicle ownership from actual trip occupancy, cargo, distance, terrain, and available alternatives. A household may reasonably own a larger vehicle for children, mobility devices, tools, equipment, severe weather, or occasional cargo while using a smaller option for ordinary commuting.

blocks.label: Section 4
blocks.text: Passenger Vehicle Classification System

rows: One- or two-person enclosed microcars, neighborhood electric vehicles, lightweight protected commuter vehicles, and approved three-wheelers
Highest right-sizing incentives and compact infrastructure priority
Small highway-capable automobiles using low road and parking space
Ordinary automobiles and crossovers for general household use
Large sport utility vehicles, passenger trucks, vans, and vehicles exceeding size or mass thresholds
Eligible for secondary commuter incentives; possible congestion contribution where alternatives exist
Vehicles justified by disability, large families, occupation, remote conditions, emergency, or specialized terrain

blocks.label: Section 5
blocks.text: National Right-Sized Commuter Program

A voluntary program shall provide persons who ordinarily commute alone with access to smaller, less expensive, and more efficient transportation. Owners of Class IV vehicles shall be prioritized but shall not be required to surrender the larger vehicle.

blocks.label: Section 6
blocks.text: Secondary Commuter Vehicle Incentive

items: Direct purchase credits or zero-interest public financing for qualifying Class I or II vehicles
Reduced registration, toll, parking, and charging costs
Employer mobility benefits and preferred compact parking
Income-based assistance where expected public savings exceed subsidy cost
Verification based on aggregate commute patterns rather than continuous location surveillance

blocks.label: Section 7
blocks.text: Vehicle Right-Sizing Exchange

A person permanently replacing a large vehicle may exchange it for a qualifying microvehicle, compact car, transit account, electric-bicycle and transit package, shared-mobility package, or other approved arrangement. Functional vehicles shall be resold, repurposed, converted, assigned to legitimate public use, or disassembled for material recovery rather than destroyed.

blocks.label: Section 8
blocks.text: Microvehicle Development
The Agency for Technological Advancement and Human Safety shall develop a transportation category between exposed motorcycles and conventional automobiles.

items: Enclosed weather protection and highway capability
Crash protection, automatic emergency braking, and collision avoidance
Lightweight repairable structures and standardized batteries
Compact parking, low-cost production, and long service life
Accessible designs and domestic manufacture

blocks.label: Section 9
blocks.text: National Commuter Vehicle Standard

Highest-level incentives shall require measured safety, low road-space use, low material demand, low energy use, repairability, parts availability, affordable lifecycle cost, and no unnecessary luxury specification. Multiple propulsion technologies may qualify.

blocks.label: Section 10
blocks.text: Affordable Commuter Vehicle Initiative

Public support shall prioritize models affordable to ordinary households. A manufacturer receiving substantial public assistance shall offer at least one qualifying commuter vehicle below a national affordability threshold based on median income, manufacturing cost, operating savings, maintenance, and lifespan.

blocks.label: Section 11
blocks.text: Domestic Manufacturing and Supplier Independence

The Nation shall maintain domestic capacity for microvehicles, compact vehicles, motors, efficient engines where necessary, batteries, tires, controls, glass, safety systems, charging equipment, and replacement parts. No strategy shall depend permanently on one foreign country, battery chemistry, corporation, or proprietary platform.

blocks.label: Section 12
blocks.text: Repairability and Offline Operation

Passenger vehicles shall comply with right-to-repair and planned-obsolescence standards. Critical driving functions shall remain usable when subscriptions, remote servers, or commercial software support end. Diagnostic information, service documentation, replacement components, and safe battery repair shall remain available.

blocks.label: Section 13
blocks.text: Single-Occupant Efficiency Zones

Heavily congested corridors may be designated only after evidence of persistent overload and reasonable alternatives. Efficient vehicles, buses, rail connections, carpools, motorcycles, bicycles, and emergency vehicles may receive dedicated lanes, reduced tolls, reserved parking, or priority routing.

blocks.label: Section 14
blocks.text: Large-Vehicle Commuter Contribution

A locally defined contribution may apply to repeated Class IV single-occupant commuting only in high-congestion areas where affordable and practical alternatives exist. It shall consider vehicle mass, road space, frequency, congestion impact, and actual availability of alternatives, not purchase price. Revenue shall remain dedicated to transportation improvement.

blocks.label: Section 15
blocks.text: No Penalty for Occasional Use

Occasional solo use of a large household vehicle for emergencies, long-distance travel, severe weather, cargo, recreation, or irregular needs shall not trigger a penalty. Regulation shall focus on repeated patterns producing measurable public burden and shall not require approval before each trip.

blocks.label: Section 16
blocks.text: Transportation Privacy

The government shall not establish continuous national vehicle-location tracking. Voluntary incentives may use odometer readings, aggregate mileage, employer certification, anonymous counts, or other minimally intrusive proof. Transportation data shall not be repurposed to infer political, religious, medical, or relational activity.

blocks.label: Section 17
blocks.text: Privacy-Preserving Occupancy Verification

Occupancy technology used for high-occupancy benefits shall establish passenger count without identifying passengers. Facial recognition, biometric identification, and transmission of passenger identity to government databases are not required.

blocks.label: Section 18
blocks.text: Employer Commuter Responsibility

items: Transit and rail passes, vanpools, shuttles, carpool matching, and compact-vehicle assistance
Secure bicycle and microvehicle storage and charging
Staggered schedules and remote work where operationally practical
Guaranteed emergency transportation
Accommodation for workers outside ordinary transit hours

blocks.label: Section 19
blocks.text: Employment Center Transportation Districts

Major campuses, hospitals, universities, industrial centers, transportation hubs, and government complexes may be required to coordinate commuting, shift changes, transit, parking, freight separation, pedestrian safety, and emergency mobility instead of transferring the entire burden to surrounding communities.

blocks.label: Section 20
blocks.text: Parking Reform

Public parking shall be designed and priced according to physical space used. Multiple microvehicles may occupy a space formerly reserved for one conventional vehicle. Compact and right-sized vehicles may receive lower public parking charges.

blocks.label: Section 21
blocks.text: Housing and Station Integration

New housing shall include secure parking and charging for right-sized vehicles and bicycles. Excessive conventional parking mandates shall not raise affordable-housing cost where transit or smaller vehicles can meet demand. Rail and transit hubs shall offer microvehicle parking, shared fleets, repair stations, and convenient transfers.

blocks.label: Section 22
blocks.text: Rural Transportation

Rural standards shall account for long distance, severe weather, unpaved roads, equipment, and limited transit. The Nation shall support efficient all-weather vehicles, modular cargo systems, demand-responsive service, shared transportation, and regional hubs rather than applying urban assumptions.

blocks.label: Section 23
blocks.text: Family, Caregiver, and Disability Accommodation

Persons regularly transporting children, older persons, disabled persons, medical equipment, or mobility devices shall receive need-based classification and exemptions. A household shall not be deemed inefficient merely because some trips occur alone.

blocks.label: Section 24
blocks.text: Occupational Vehicle Exemption

A larger vehicle used to transport tools, materials, equipment, or merchandise necessary for lawful work may qualify as specialized. Documentation shall be simple and based on actual functional need rather than business ownership alone.

blocks.label: Section 25
blocks.text: Vehicle Sharing and Community Reserves

Public, cooperative, nonprofit, and regulated commercial sharing systems shall allow households to use a small daily vehicle while obtaining vans, pickup trucks, accessible vehicles, long-distance vehicles, or cargo capacity only when needed. Community Vehicle Reserves shall use transparent pricing and nondiscriminatory access.

blocks.label: Section 26
blocks.text: Insurance Reform

Insurance shall recognize lower exposure where a large household vehicle is used occasionally and a smaller vehicle performs daily commuting. Multi-vehicle mobility policies shall price actual mileage, risk, mass, repair cost, and use without requiring continuous location tracking.

blocks.label: Section 27
blocks.text: Registration Reform

Registration may consider mass, road space, energy use, mileage, emissions, and purpose. A household materially reducing use of a Class IV vehicle through a Class I or II commuter vehicle may pay a lower combined registration cost than the previous cost of the large vehicle alone.

blocks.label: Section 28
blocks.text: Technology-Neutral Incentives

Support shall reflect total energy, mass, materials, repairability, road wear, lifecycle emissions, and infrastructure. A very heavy electric vehicle shall not automatically receive more public benefit than a substantially lighter efficient vehicle solely because it lacks tailpipe emissions.

blocks.label: Section 29
blocks.text: Vehicle Mass and Public Safety

Safety standards shall address collision compatibility, pedestrian danger, visibility, hood height, braking, tire pollution, road wear, and crash energy transferred to smaller road users. Manufacturers shall not increase mass beyond functional need merely to transfer risk outward.

blocks.label: Section 30
blocks.text: Transportation Resource Label

items: Vehicle mass, passenger and cargo capacity, and road-space footprint
Energy use, tire consumption, maintenance, and operating cost per mile
Expected useful life and repairability score
Material recovery score and comparison with vehicles serving a similar purpose

blocks.label: Section 31
blocks.text: National Transportation Awareness Program

Public education shall explain measurable effects of vehicle size on energy, roads, parking, congestion, depreciation, insurance, collisions, material use, and alternatives without describing lawful large-vehicle ownership as immoral.

blocks.label: Section 32
blocks.text: National Right-Sized Transportation Challenge

Universities, students, manufacturers, engineers, municipalities, laboratories, cooperatives, and independent inventors may compete to develop safer and more affordable personal transportation. Evaluation shall include purchase price, operating cost, weather protection, accessibility, domestic manufacturability, repairability, road space, material use, and quality of life.

blocks.label: Section 33
blocks.text: Pilot Transportation Communities

Voluntary communities may test microvehicle networks, compact parking, shared fleets, employer districts, automated shuttles, dynamic road allocation, and rail integration. Costs, safety, satisfaction, privacy, and performance shall be public, and unsuccessful systems shall end.

blocks.label: Section 34
blocks.text: Dedicated Transportation Revenue

Transportation charges shall remain dedicated to roads, bridges, transit, rail integration, microvehicle infrastructure, pedestrian and bicycle safety, research, manufacturing, and emergency mobility rather than becoming unrestricted general revenue.

blocks.label: Section 35
blocks.text: No Forced Disposal and Transition

No person shall be required to destroy or surrender a functional lawful vehicle solely because a smaller option exists. Transition shall use voluntary incentives, fleet turnover, sharing, right-sizing exchanges, and infrastructure reform.

blocks.label: Section 36
blocks.text: National Action Records, Measurement, and Review

Every major incentive, restriction, classification, congestion program, exchange, subsidy, or infrastructure project shall possess a National Action Record. ONPA shall measure occupancy, mass per passenger, energy and road space per passenger-mile, household cost, congestion, commute time, road and parking cost, safety, adoption, rural access, manufacturing, lifespan, privacy, and public satisfaction. Restrictions shall expire where affordable alternatives or measurable benefit do not exist.

blocks.label: Section 37
blocks.text: Final Standard

Large vehicles shall remain available where their capabilities are useful. Small vehicles, shared vehicles, rail, transit, bicycles, and other efficient systems shall become easier and cheaper for trips where additional size provides no meaningful benefit. Success is right-sized choice without sacrifice of safety, independence, convenience, affordability, or constitutional freedom.

articles.summary: Passenger transportation shall preserve personal mobility while reducing unnecessary consumption of energy, road capacity, materials, land, parking, and household income. The routine transportation of one person in a vehicle substantially larger and heavier than necessary imposes avoidable congestion, road wear, collision energy, tire and brake pollution, and infrastructure cost.

articles.title: Social Media Access, Child Protection, Platform Accountability, And User Autonomy

blocks.label: Section 1
blocks.text: Constitutional Purpose

Social media may serve communication, creativity, association, education, commerce, journalism, and public discussion. Because platforms influence attention, development, behavior, identity, and public knowledge at population scale, they carry heightened duties of child protection, authentication, privacy, content safety, transparency, and user autonomy.
Regulation shall not authorize political censorship or punishment of lawful criticism, religion, journalism, satire, science, art, or ordinary disagreement.

blocks.label: Section 2
blocks.text: Definition of Social Media Platform

A covered platform is a service whose principal or substantial function permits public or semi-public accounts, user-generated content, following, audience accumulation, algorithmic recommendations, livestreaming, or public and group interaction. Functional reality controls over labels such as profile, channel, creator page, gaming identity, or community account.

blocks.label: Section 3
blocks.text: Minimum Account Age

No person under sixteen may create, control, maintain, or independently use a social-media account. Child access to isolated public information that requires no account may remain available where lawful and developmentally appropriate. Platforms shall not pressure account creation through schools, games, entertainment, or retail services.

blocks.label: Section 4
blocks.text: Platform Duty to Verify Age

A self-entered date of birth is insufficient. Each platform shall use a proportionate Age Assurance System combining privacy-preserving credentials, verified birthdate, mobile verification, device authentication, trusted third-party confirmation, biometric age assurance where lawful, anomaly detection, periodic re-verification, and human review. The platform bears primary responsibility for accuracy.

blocks.label: Section 5
blocks.text: Mandatory Two-Factor Authentication

Every account shall use a verified mobile telephone number and a device-based Face ID or equivalent biometric presence check, together with a secure recovery method. Raw facial scans and reusable biometric templates shall remain on the user device where technically practical and shall not be retained by the platform. Equally secure alternatives shall exist for disability, medical limitation, incompatible hardware, or other legitimate barriers.

blocks.label: Section 6
blocks.text: Public Pseudonymity and Confidential Verification

Verification shall not require publication of a legal name. Users may use pseudonyms, artistic identities, organizational names, or anonymous public-facing identifiers while the platform maintains confidential accountability records for serious misconduct. Verification data shall not be sold or used for advertising.

blocks.label: Section 7
blocks.text: Detection and Immediate Restriction of Underage Accounts

Platforms shall actively detect probable under-sixteen users through lawful indicators and human review. Credible evidence shall trigger immediate restriction of posting, messaging, purchases, livestreaming, recommendations, and account replacement pending prompt review. Mistaken classification shall have a correction process.

blocks.label: Section 8
blocks.text: Mandatory Response Upon Verification

items: Suspend the account and prevent replacement by the same verified user
Preserve records necessary for investigation while protecting the child from public identification
Notify a lawful guardian where safe and authorized
Report to the National Digital Platform Safety Authority
Remove unlawfully collected child data
Complete a root-cause review and correct the verification failure

blocks.label: Section 9
blocks.text: Civil Penalties for Underage Accounts

Each of the first ten independently verified children under sixteen present on a platform during the applicable enforcement period shall create a civil penalty of two hundred fifty thousand dollars per child. One child ordinarily constitutes one violation even if multiple accounts existed, while repeated accounts may aggravate the finding of system failure.

rows: $250,000 civil penalty per child, public safety review, corrective order, and disclosure
Mandatory platform-shutdown proceeding and immediate restrictions pending judicial determination
Individual criminal investigation, executive liability, compensation clawback, and enhanced corporate penalties

blocks.label: Section 10
blocks.text: No Imprisonment for Corporate Inability to Pay

A corporation’s inability to pay a civil fine shall not automatically convert the fine into imprisonment. Jail or imprisonment requires proof beyond a reasonable doubt that an identifiable person knowingly concealed children, falsified records, destroyed evidence, disabled safeguards, obstructed investigation, or continued unlawful child access for profit.

blocks.label: Section 11
blocks.text: Mandatory Shutdown Proceeding After More Than Ten

More than ten independently verified children shall trigger a petition to the National Commercial Court. The court may suspend new accounts, temporarily suspend operation, remove executives, install independent administration, require redesign, separate compliant divisions, order sale, or permanently terminate operating authority. Permanent shutdown requires a finding that violations were systemic, knowingly tolerated, structurally unavoidable, or not correctable through lesser means.

blocks.label: Section 12
blocks.text: Enforcement Period and Successor Liability

The ordinary counting period is one year and may extend to three years for concealment or delayed discovery. Renaming, restructuring, asset transfer, related-company sale, or migration to a substantially identical service shall not reset the count. Successors inherit unresolved obligations.

blocks.label: Section 13
blocks.text: Protection Against Manufactured Violations

No competitor, activist, individual, or organization may pay children, forge credentials, compromise accounts, use synthetic identities, organize mass circumvention, or submit knowingly false reports for the purpose of imposing penalties or shutting down a platform. A platform may defend a manufactured incident only by proving compliant systems, prompt response, absence of participation, no profit after notice, and immediate reporting. Weak verification is not excused.

blocks.label: Section 14
blocks.text: Independent Verification and Due Process

No reported child counts toward the shutdown threshold until an independent authority verifies age, account control, duration, platform response, verification records, malicious orchestration, and concealment. The platform receives notice and an opportunity to respond, while emergency restrictions may protect children before final decision.

blocks.label: Section 15
blocks.text: Users Sixteen and Seventeen

Users aged sixteen or seventeen may create accounts only with enhanced protection. Platforms shall not use personal data, inferred interests, emotional state, location, social relationships, private communication, or behavior to target advertisements, promotions, sponsored content, or commercial solicitations. Only clearly labeled, age-appropriate, non-personalized contextual advertising may be shown.

blocks.label: Section 16
blocks.text: Prohibited Promotions to Minors

items: Gambling, alcohol, nicotine, recreational drugs, and products prohibited to minors
Credit cards, high-interest credit, financial speculation, and distress-based financial offers
Adult sexual services and exploitative cosmetic or dieting products
Behavioral promotions exploiting body insecurity, grief, anxiety, family conflict, or mental distress
Influencer placements and paid recommendations that are not clearly identified as advertising

blocks.label: Section 17
blocks.text: Universal Right to Disable Advertising

Every user may disable all commercial advertisements and targeted promotions without cost. The choice shall not reduce posting, messaging, search, performance, content quality, account visibility, storage, safety, saved content, upload limits, or ordinary support. Legally required notices and public-safety information are not advertising.

blocks.label: Section 18
blocks.text: Prohibition of Paid Ad-Avoidance Tiers

A platform shall not create a paid tier whose principal purpose is removing ads, restoring privacy, providing a chronological feed, restoring ordinary functionality, or avoiding commercial tracking. Privacy and freedom from advertising are baseline rights. A paid tier may offer genuine additional services unrelated to reversing an imposed burden.

blocks.label: Section 19
blocks.text: Targeted Advertising Opt-Out and Non-Retaliation

Every user may prohibit the use of browsing, location, contacts, messages, health, politics, religion, finances, emotional state, biometrics, or off-platform activity for commercial targeting. The setting remains active until affirmatively changed and shall not be reset by new terms, devices, updates, or reinstallation. Platforms may not reduce reach or functionality because of privacy choices.

blocks.label: Section 20
blocks.text: Sexual Content on General Platforms

Platforms not principally dedicated to lawful adult sexual content shall prohibit pornography and explicit sexual content. Medical, educational, legal, historical, journalistic, abuse-prevention, and necessary artistic material may be allowed with context and restrictions. Discussion of sexuality, health, identity, or reproductive medicine is not pornography.

blocks.label: Section 21
blocks.text: Adult-Purpose Platforms

A platform whose sole principal purpose is lawful adult sexual content shall operate separately, exclude all persons under eighteen, verify performer age and consent, prohibit trafficking, nonconsensual imagery, hidden-camera material, and child sexual abuse material, and maintain heightened privacy, cybersecurity, and removal systems.

blocks.label: Section 22
blocks.text: Absolute Prohibition of Child Sexual Material

Any sexual depiction of a person under eighteen is prohibited regardless of claimed consent, artificial generation, manipulation, animation, or platform classification. Platforms shall remove, preserve evidence, and report suspected material without redistributing it for commentary or awareness.

blocks.label: Section 23
blocks.text: Killing, Torture, and Gore

Actual killing, torture, dismemberment, execution, severe mutilation, and extreme gore shall be prohibited from ordinary feeds, monetization, replay, recommendation, and entertainment. Accounts knowingly posting or livestreaming such content shall be immediately suspended and ordinarily permanently banned. Evidence of crime shall be preserved.

blocks.label: Section 24
blocks.text: Limited Evidentiary and Public-Interest Exceptions

Restricted access may exist for courts, criminal investigation, verified journalism, human-rights documentation, medical education, or historical preservation. Such material shall require warnings, purposeful access, and noncommercial context and shall never enter ordinary recommendations.

blocks.label: Section 25
blocks.text: Livestream Safety

Platforms permitting livestreaming shall maintain real-time systems capable of interrupting apparent killing, suicide, torture, sexual violence, child exploitation, hostage-taking, or immediate severe threats. High-risk streams shall receive qualified human review and emergency escalation without treating ordinary demonstrations or journalism as prohibited merely because conflict occurs.

blocks.label: Section 26
blocks.text: Suicide and Self-Harm

Content instructing, encouraging, celebrating, or competitively promoting suicide or serious self-harm is prohibited. Recovery, prevention, personal experience, journalism, and medical education remain lawful. Emergency intervention shall be narrow and shall not create permanent commercial or governmental behavioral surveillance.

blocks.label: Section 27
blocks.text: Moderation Duty and Staffing

Moderation capacity shall be proportionate to users, content volume, languages, and risk. Automated systems shall assist but shall not possess final authority over complex constitutional questions without human appeal. Chronic understaffing is not a defense. Moderators shall be trained in child exploitation, graphic violence, suicide, trafficking, evidence preservation, cultural context, disability, satire, journalism, and lawful political speech.

blocks.label: Section 28
blocks.text: Public Rules, Notice, and Appeal

Platforms shall publish ordinary-language rules on age, content, advertising, authentication, data, enforcement, appeals, preservation, and referrals. Except in immediate emergency cases, users shall receive notice, the rule invoked, evidence description, duration, and appeal. Permanent bans require human review by personnel independent of the original decision.

blocks.label: Section 29
blocks.text: Algorithmic Feed Control

Every user may select a chronological or other non-personalized feed without cost or degradation. Users aged sixteen or seventeen shall receive a non-personalized feed by default unless limited personalization is affirmatively selected under national safeguards.

blocks.label: Section 30
blocks.text: Compulsive Design Restrictions for Minors

Platforms shall not direct infinite scrolling without stopping points, streak-loss threats, variable rewards, artificial urgency, nighttime re-engagement, public popularity pressure, or autoplay designed to prevent disengagement toward users aged sixteen or seventeen where the foreseeable effect is compulsive use.

blocks.label: Section 31
blocks.text: Nighttime Protection for Minors

Minor accounts shall enter a default reduced-interruption mode during local nighttime hours. Nonessential notifications, autoplay, commercial promotions, livestream prompts, and active re-engagement shall stop, while deliberate access remains possible.

blocks.label: Section 32
blocks.text: Minor Data Minimization

Platforms shall not collect or infer precise location history, emotional state, mental-health status, sexual interests, family conflict, finances, school performance, politics, religion, private relationship patterns, or unnecessary biometrics for commercial use involving users sixteen or seventeen. Minor data shall not be sold.

blocks.label: Section 33
blocks.text: Quarterly Child Safety Compliance Record

items: Suspected and independently verified underage accounts
Detection method, removal time, appeals, and restorations
Commercial data collected before removal and known adult-contact incidents
Verification failures, penalties, corrective measures, and management responsibility
Aggregate reporting that does not identify children

blocks.label: Section 34
blocks.text: Public Scrutiny and Hearings

A penalized platform shall enter the Public Digital Safety Review Registry and may be required to explain verification failure, duration, advertising, adult contact, collected data, internal warnings, management decisions, and corrections at public hearings. Children and families shall not be turned into public evidence displays without consent.

blocks.label: Section 35
blocks.text: National Digital Platform Safety Authority

An independent Authority shall certify age systems, investigate child access, audit authentication, enforce advertising and content standards, hear complaints, protect whistleblowers, publish compliance records, and petition courts for suspension or shutdown. It shall not control lawful political ideology.

blocks.label: Section 36
blocks.text: Independent Audits and Whistleblower Protection

Major platforms shall undergo independent audits funded through regulated assessments without platform selection of the auditor. Employees, contractors, researchers, and moderators disclosing credible safety failures, manipulated metrics, dangerous recommendations, or unlawful practices shall be protected from retaliation and abusive secrecy claims.

blocks.label: Section 37
blocks.text: Guardian and User Reporting

Guardians shall receive expedited confidential reporting for underage accounts. All users shall have simple tools for exploitation, pornography, gore, coercion, threats, impersonation, and authentication abuse. Knowingly false or coordinated reports intended to silence lawful users or manufacture violations may result in account and legal consequences.

blocks.label: Section 38
blocks.text: Portability and Purchased Digital Property

Users may export posts, media, lawful messages, connections, preferences, purchases, and user-created data. Account suspension shall not automatically revoke separately purchased lawful digital property, which remains protected under the Consumer Services and Digital Property Article.

blocks.label: Section 39
blocks.text: Shutdown and Consumer Continuity

Before permanent shutdown, the court shall establish a plan for data export, purchased content, refunds, recurring-payment termination, evidence, employee transition, creator compensation, privacy, and lawful archival preservation. Executives may not erase evidence or transfer assets to evade obligations.

blocks.label: Section 40
blocks.text: Executive Accountability and Penalty Use

Personal liability requires knowledge, direction, reckless disregard, concealment, or failure to act after credible notice. Penalties shall primarily support child safety, victims, literacy, research, audits, and privacy-preserving verification. Insolvency priority shall protect endangered children, employees, consumer refunds, and data obligations before owners.

blocks.label: Section 41
blocks.text: Protection of Lawful Speech

Nothing in this Article permits suppression solely because content is offensive, unpopular, political, religious, critical, scientific, artistic, or controversial. Regulation focuses on unlawful child access, exploitation, explicit pornography on general platforms, actual extreme violence, threats, commercial manipulation, and clearly defined safety duties.

blocks.label: Section 42
blocks.text: Research and Social Media Literacy

Qualified researchers may access controlled de-identified data to study child exposure, mental health, advertising, addictive design, harassment, exploitation, misinformation, and moderation. A national literacy program shall teach privacy, targeting, impersonation, manipulation, exploitation, authentication, and reporting without requiring children under sixteen to create accounts.

blocks.label: Section 43
blocks.text: National Action Records and Independent Measurement

Every major age, advertising, authentication, content, suspension, and technical rule shall possess a National Action Record addressing evidence, privacy, speech, cost, benefit, error, sabotage, review, and expiration. ONPA shall measure underage prevalence, verification accuracy, false suspensions, advertising, harmful content removal, privacy, authentication, shutdown proceedings, lawful speech effects, competition, and user well-being.

blocks.label: Section 44
blocks.text: Transition and Final Standard

Existing platforms shall comply within two years through account review, uncertain-account re-verification, phone and device-local biometric authentication, removal of underage users, free ad opt-out, minor-targeting prohibition, moderation strengthening, reporting, and independent audit.

Children under sixteen shall not be treated as users, advertising audiences, behavioral datasets, or sources of engagement. Minor users shall receive enhanced protection, adults shall retain lawful speech and privacy, and platform scale shall never become immunity from accountability.

articles.summary: Social media may serve communication, creativity, association, education, commerce, journalism, and public discussion. Because platforms influence attention, development, behavior, identity, and public knowledge at population scale, they carry heightened duties of child protection, authentication, privacy, content safety, transparency, and user autonomy.

articles.title: The Automated Industrial Transition, Human Sovereignty, And Post-Scarcity Civil Continuity

blocks.label: Section 1
blocks.text: Recognition of the New Industrial Revolution

The combined development of artificial intelligence, robotics, autonomous manufacturing, advanced computation, synthetic biology, additive manufacturing, distributed energy, and related technologies constitutes a new Industrial Revolution. Machines will increasingly perform work once requiring human labor, analysis, coordination, and continuous presence.

The Nation shall not preserve unnecessary labor solely because employment once depended on it, nor permit productivity to create mass deprivation, permanent exclusion, concentration of essential resources, or replacement of human constitutional judgment.

blocks.label: Section 2
blocks.text: Long-Term Constitutional Objective

Automation shall reduce involuntary labor, material scarcity, preventable illness, housing insecurity, transportation burden, food insecurity, resource waste, and dependence on fragile centralized systems. Every person shall retain reliable access to housing, food, water, sanitation, energy, healthcare, education, communications, transportation, and essential goods.

blocks.label: Section 3
blocks.text: Preservation of the Constitutional Government

The five-cohort Executive Council, Assembly of State Delegations, courts, rights, National Action Records, ONPA, and distributed institutions shall not be altered, suspended, or transferred to artificial intelligence or unelected technical systems merely because the workforce changes. Machines shall not redefine cohorts, office eligibility, representation, judicial authority, rights, elections, succession, emergency power, or amendment procedure.

blocks.label: Section 4
blocks.text: General Civil Appeal for Structural Alteration

Fundamental structural change may proceed only through a General Civil Appeal based on a nationwide petition stating the full proposal, every affected Article, transition needs, risks, and power consequences. The proposal shall remain public for at least one year and receive independent legal, technical, economic, and human-rights review.

items: An aggregate national majority cannot substitute for approval in every cohort.
Failure in any cohort defeats the proposal.
Synthetic identities, automated petitions, coercion, hidden behavioral targeting, and purchased support invalidate affected records.

blocks.label: Section 5
blocks.text: Protection Against Automated Constitutional Capture

Artificial intelligence systems, corporations, foreign governments, political organizations, and automated campaigns shall not manipulate a General Civil Appeal through synthetic identities, forged signatures, mass automation, workplace coercion, concealed sponsorship, or undisclosed personalized persuasion.

blocks.label: Section 6
blocks.text: National Automated Transition Authority

An independent National Automated Transition Authority shall coordinate labor displacement, transition income, public ownership, retraining, automation dividends, distributed production, and long-term social adaptation. It shall complement, not replace, the National Agency for Technological Advancement and Human Safety.

Workforce Displacement Forecast

blocks.label: Section 7
blocks.text: Automation Classification

rows: Technology increases human capability while humans retain responsibility and judgment.
Defined tasks are automated while humans retain the occupation’s primary authority.
Most routine functions are automated while humans provide supervision, exceptions, policy judgment, and accountability.
An industrial or service function operates with minimal ordinary human labor.
Technology is proposed for government, courts, law enforcement, military, elections, regulation, or coercive public authority and is subject to the highest restrictions.

blocks.label: Section 8
blocks.text: Automation Impact Assessment

Before major automation materially reduces a workforce, the organization shall publish jobs and tasks affected, timing, productivity, public benefit, displaced wages, retraining, regional effects, concentration, cybersecurity, continuity, environment, and distribution of gain. Ordinary layoffs shall not be falsely labeled technological necessity.

blocks.label: Section 9
blocks.text: Transition Notice

Large-scale displacement ordinarily requires not less than twelve months of notice and longer where a regional industry, specialized retraining, relocation, or essential services are involved. Workers shall receive timelines, affected functions, training, income continuation, transfer opportunities, and a right to challenge materially false assessments.

blocks.label: Section 10
blocks.text: Shared Productivity and Displacement Compensation

Where automation increases productivity by reducing labor, a defined share of the gain shall return to affected workers and the public through transition income, reduced hours without proportional pay loss, employee ownership, profit distribution, retraining, community investment, or public automation contributions.

blocks.label: Section 11
blocks.text: National Automation Dividend

A National Automation Dividend shall be funded through extraordinary automation-derived profits, publicly owned automated production, public equity in publicly financed technology, licensing of public technology, infrastructure savings, and recovered resources. Its purpose is universal essential access and reduced dependence on currency, not permanent dependence on private monopolies.

blocks.label: Section 12
blocks.text: Reduction of the Standard Work Requirement

Where national output can be maintained with less labor, policy shall reduce the workweek, share employment, expand education, caregiving, civic contribution, science, art, community production, and sustainable earlier retirement rather than requiring a small employed population to work excessive hours.

blocks.label: Section 13
blocks.text: Transition Income

A worker displaced by certified automation shall receive income sufficient for housing, food, healthcare, transportation, communications, and family stability for a period based on prior service, age, regional employment, training duration, disability, caregiving, and occupational elimination. Support may decline when stable income exists or reasonable opportunities are refused without justification, but emergency essentials remain protected.

blocks.label: Section 14
blocks.text: Worker Ownership and Training Data Rights

Employees whose labor, procedures, operational knowledge, decisions, or accumulated experience materially trained or enabled a replacement system may receive ownership, royalty, dividend, or productivity rights. Organizations shall not secretly extract employee data to build a replacement system without disclosure and lawful authorization.

blocks.label: Section 15
blocks.text: Education for the Automated Age

items: Artificial intelligence literacy, robotics, programming, cybersecurity, systems design, and engineering
Advanced manufacturing, maintenance, repair, data interpretation, and biological sciences
Ethical reasoning, constitutional responsibility, interpersonal communication, and creative production
Preparation to understand, supervise, challenge, improve, and govern machines rather than train solely for disappearing occupations

blocks.label: Section 16
blocks.text: Adult Transition Education

Every adult materially displaced by automation shall receive access to public technical institutes, universities, employers, cooperatives, online systems, regional manufacturing centers, infrastructure projects, and National Challenges. Programs must lead to real productive capability rather than repeated low-value attendance.

blocks.label: Section 17
blocks.text: Human-Safeguarded Positions of Power

Final authority in elected office, courts, juries, prosecution, arrest, sentencing, parole, military command, lethal-force authorization, election certification, emergency declaration, constitutional interpretation, regulatory enforcement, and compulsory placement shall remain with identifiable human beings. Artificial intelligence may advise but shall not decide liberty, rights, legal status, or political representation.

blocks.label: Section 18
blocks.text: Law Enforcement and Coercive Technology

No autonomous machine shall independently arrest, determine guilt, interrogate, impose punishment, or use lethal force. Narrow automated defensive action may occur only during an immediate threat to life under pre-established rules, complete audit, human responsibility, and mandatory review.

blocks.label: Section 19
blocks.text: Automated Public Administration

Routine benefits, licensing, infrastructure scheduling, information, logistics, fraud detection, and procurement analysis may be automated where notice, explanation, correction, and human appeal remain available. No essential benefit, housing, healthcare, education, vote, or legal standing shall be denied by an unreviewed automated decision.

blocks.label: Section 20
blocks.text: Public Ownership of Essential Automated Capacity

The Nation, states, municipalities, cooperatives, and nonprofit institutions shall maintain sufficient automated capacity for food, water, sanitation, energy, housing components, medicine, communications, transportation parts, clothing, repair parts, and emergency supplies. No population shall depend completely on one private network for survival.

blocks.label: Section 21
blocks.text: Prevention of Automated Monopoly

No organization shall hold permanent exclusive control over essential AI models, robotics, compute, energy, manufacturing, agriculture, medicine, additive manufacturing, digital identity, logistics, essential software, or raw-material processing. Remedies may include interoperability, public licensing, separation, public acquisition, and community production rights.

blocks.label: Section 22
blocks.text: National Distributed Production Network

Municipal fabrication centers, regional robotics facilities, public 3D printing, automated food production, repair cooperatives, machine shops, material recovery, and mobile units shall produce essential goods near the communities that use them and preserve operation during international or national disruption.

blocks.label: Section 23
blocks.text: Right to Fabricate Lawful Goods

People shall receive reasonable access to additive manufacturing for ordinary tools, household goods, repair parts, educational materials, and accessibility devices. Public centers shall provide certified design files, safety information, repair instructions, quality standards, training, and affordable materials. Weapons, dangerous chemicals, fraudulent safety components, counterfeit goods, and products creating substantial danger remain restricted.

blocks.label: Section 24
blocks.text: National Public Design Repository

Publicly financed designs for repair parts, infrastructure, medical accessories, agriculture, sanitation, emergency equipment, education, and household goods shall ordinarily be available for public production with material specifications, safety ratings, lifespan, repair instructions, and recovery classification. Critical files shall remain available offline.

blocks.label: Section 25
blocks.text: Digital Design Ownership and Continuity

A one-time purchased digital manufacturing design remains permanent digital property. A platform closure, subscription termination, or newer model shall not revoke it. Repair designs necessary for the expected life of a product shall remain available, and discontinued manufacturers shall release necessary designs where required for lawful continued use and safety.

blocks.label: Section 26
blocks.text: Automated Food Production

Distributed controlled-environment agriculture, robotic farming, fermentation, cultivated ingredients, automated kitchens, and food-printing systems may reduce labor and transport. Widespread deployment requires independent review of nutrition, contamination, health, energy, environment, and supply dependency. Traditional agriculture remains protected for resilience, ecology, culture, and security.

blocks.label: Section 27
blocks.text: Convenient Printable and Automated Food Access

When mature and safe, communities shall provide access to systems producing standardized meals, ingredients, and nutrition according to verified needs. Open safety standards shall prevent dependence on one proprietary ingredient cartridge or supplier, and all ingredients, allergens, nutrition, processing, and risks shall be disclosed.

blocks.label: Section 28
blocks.text: Safe Containment of Raw Goods

Household, community, and industrial containment standards shall safely store grains, oils, proteins, ingredients, fibers, polymers, metals, biological feedstocks, and construction materials. Systems shall prevent contamination, spoilage, incompatible mixing, fire, theft, and accidental exposure while preserving material identity and future use.

blocks.label: Section 29
blocks.text: Personal and Community Resource Independence
Complete self-sufficiency shall not be compulsory. The goal is safe, affordable, interoperable independence and resilience.

items: Local energy generation and storage
Water purification, greywater reuse, and circular sanitation
Food cultivation, preservation, and automated production
Fabrication, repair equipment, raw-material storage, and local communications
Shared transportation and community resource systems

blocks.label: Section 30
blocks.text: Universal Essential Resource Floor

As automated production expands, every citizen shall receive sufficient access to basic housing, nutrition, potable water, sanitation, baseline energy, essential healthcare, communications, education, local transportation, and public fabrication independent of employment status. Additional lawful goods and services remain available.

blocks.label: Section 31
blocks.text: Currency Reduction Strategy

Currency shall not be abolished by declaration. Dependence shall decline only as direct essential access becomes universal, reliable, measurable, private, and resilient. The transition may use universal services, resource credits, public fabrication, community resources, reduced work hours, automation dividends, and gradual elimination of charges for essentials while voluntary trade, ownership, and savings continue.

blocks.label: Section 32
blocks.text: Conditions for Elimination of Currency

items: Universal and reliable Essential Resource Floor
Essential production substantially above minimum national need
Distribution resilient during disruption and free from political favoritism
Meaningful consumer choice and fair allocation of genuinely scarce resources
International exchange and personal property legally maintained
Corruption, hidden privilege, and regional deprivation independently measurable
Approval through a General Civil Appeal with 75 percent in every cohort

blocks.label: Section 33
blocks.text: Allocation in a Low-Currency Society

Scarce resources shall use transparent equal access, medical need, household size, caregiving, occupational necessity, emergency priority, production availability, and voluntary waiting systems. Former wealth, political status, ideology, government employment, and personal connections shall not create privileged access. Human appeal and audit are mandatory.

blocks.label: Section 34
blocks.text: Individual Choice and Prohibition of Social Credit

Resource security shall preserve lawful work choice, voluntary creation and exchange, cooperative organization, travel, education, art, privacy, and political difference. Essential food, housing, energy, medicine, transportation, or communication shall never depend on ideological conformity, popularity, broad behavioral scoring, political loyalty, or lawful speech.

blocks.label: Section 35
blocks.text: Human Purpose Beyond Employment

Human value is not measured solely by market employment. Caregiving, parenting, education, research, art, invention, maintenance, community service, restoration, deliberation, mentorship, and cultural preservation remain recognized forms of contribution. No person shall be officially treated as worthless because machines perform a former occupation.

blocks.label: Section 36
blocks.text: Voluntary Civic Contribution

Where essentials are secure, citizens may be encouraged to contribute limited time to preparedness, education, care, infrastructure inspection, environmental restoration, public deliberation, culture, local production, or mentorship. It shall not become indefinite forced labor, and exemptions shall protect disability, health, caregiving, age, and education.

blocks.label: Section 37
blocks.text: Human Oversight and Liability

Every high-impact AI system shall have identifiable human officials or organizations responsible for authorization, data, performance, bias, security, error, shutdown, and legal compliance. An organization shall not avoid liability by claiming that the system acted independently.

blocks.label: Section 38
blocks.text: Political Status of Artificial Intelligence

Artificial intelligence and robotic systems shall not vote, hold elected office, serve as jurors, sign General Civil Appeals, make political donations, possess human civil status, or exercise independent coercive authority. Limited legal status may exist for contracting, identification, assets, or accountability but shall not reduce human responsibility.

blocks.label: Section 39
blocks.text: Identity and Biometric Protection

Essential resources shall not require continuous biometric or location tracking. Secure alternatives shall exist where facial or other biometric systems cannot be used. No unrestricted personal model shall combine health, movement, relationships, speech, purchases, and political behavior.

blocks.label: Section 40
blocks.text: Automated Infrastructure Resilience

Essential automated systems shall retain local control, manual fallback, offline operation, spare components, human operators, independent power, open standards, and restoration procedures. Failure of one AI model, cloud provider, license, network, or authentication service shall not stop essential resources.

blocks.label: Section 41
blocks.text: Preservation of Human Technical Knowledge

Before removing a functioning human process, an organization shall prove the automated replacement can be operated, repaired, overridden, and restored. The Nation shall preserve human competence in food, water, sanitation, medicine, construction, energy, transportation, communications, manufacturing, and government even if machines perform ordinary operations.

blocks.label: Section 42
blocks.text: Total Reuse Economy and Resource Ledger

Automation shall support disassembly, component harvesting, repair, remanufacturing, recycling, biological recovery, material purification, safe containment, and redistribution. A privacy-protected resource ledger may track major flows and capacity without recording every person’s possessions or detailed consumption.

blocks.label: Section 43
blocks.text: Safe Material Containment Network

Communities shall maintain standardized containment preserving identity, purity, compatibility, access, environmental protection, emergency availability, and future recovery. Materials shall not be mixed for convenience where separation preserves value. Containment replaces disposal as the ordinary final stage.

blocks.label: Section 44
blocks.text: Public Access to Automated Production

Publicly supported manufacturing shall reserve capacity for citizens, small businesses, schools, cooperatives, emergency services, and local governments. Dominant corporations shall not purchase all capacity. Scheduling, material requirements, safety, costs, and priority during shortage shall be public.

blocks.label: Section 45
blocks.text: Continuity Cell Production Capacity

Every Continuity Cell shall plan to produce or repair water, sanitation, electrical, medical, communications, food-processing, transportation, shelter, agricultural, and emergency components sufficient for survival and initial restoration during regional isolation.

blocks.label: Section 46
blocks.text: Highly Automated Businesses and Human Governance

A highly automated company remains subject to local contribution, consumer protection, material circulation, continuity, and public-benefit obligations even if few employees remain. It shall maintain identifiable human directors and accountability officers; AI cannot be the sole executive, board, compliance officer, or legal representative.

blocks.label: Section 47
blocks.text: Public Employment During Transition

Where private employment disappears faster than universal resources expand, public employment shall build housing, rail, sanitation, environmental restoration, caregiving, education, research, material recovery, emergency capacity, and community production. Obsolete tasks shall not be preserved merely to occupy people.

blocks.label: Section 48
blocks.text: National Transition Phases

rows: Map capability, workforce exposure, dependencies, and regional vulnerability; begin notice and safeguards.
Distribute gains through shorter hours, transition income, worker ownership, public contributions, and local investment.
Make housing, food, water, sanitation, energy, health, communications, education, and transport increasingly independent of employment.
Expand community fabrication, food systems, energy independence, material recovery, and raw-resource containment.
Reduce currency dependence as direct essential access becomes universal and reliable.
Consider complete currency elimination or fundamental restructuring only through General Civil Appeal.

blocks.label: Section 49
blocks.text: Milestones, Pilots, and Scientific Review

The Authority shall publish milestones for displacement, work hours, transition income, dividends, ownership, essential access, manufacturing, food, energy, water, reuse, human control, regional equality, and satisfaction. Pilot communities may test reduced workweeks, dividends, public fabrication, automated food, resource credits, and low-currency systems with rights, exit procedures, and independent review.

blocks.label: Section 50
blocks.text: Public Transparency, Action Records, and Measurement

Annual reports shall distinguish verified outcomes from forecasts and disclose displacement, jobs, productivity, wages, hours, ownership, revenue, regional impact, concentration, resource access, failures, security, oversight, and environment. Every major automated program shall possess a National Action Record. ONPA shall determine whether automation increases freedom and broad access or merely concentrates control.

blocks.label: Section 51
blocks.text: Enforcement, Citizen Appeal, and Judicial Review

Violations may produce worker restitution, extended transition income, ownership grants, suspension, mandatory human oversight, interoperability, public access, compensation clawbacks, probation, public acquisition of essential capacity, blacklisting, or criminal prosecution for deliberate concealment or unlawful harm. Citizens shall receive human review of displacement, resources, privacy, biometric, and automated administrative disputes. Courts retain final constitutional authority.

blocks.label: Section 52
blocks.text: Final Constitutional Standard

The new Industrial Revolution shall be judged by human freedom, security, health, knowledge, independence, dignity, time, and universal access rather than the number of jobs eliminated or private profit created. Machines may calculate, construct, manufacture, diagnose, transport, and assist; human beings remain sovereign. A post-currency society is not complete until benefits are universal, systems are resilient, power is human-accountable, and every cohort has consented to fundamental change.

articles.summary: The combined development of artificial intelligence, robotics, autonomous manufacturing, advanced computation, synthetic biology, additive manufacturing, distributed energy, and related technologies constitutes a new Industrial Revolution. blocks.text: All authority under this Constitution is held in trust for the people. Every person exercising public power shall preserve the liberties, institutions, records, resources, knowledge, and continuity of the Commonwealth; shall disclose conflicts; shall accept measurement and review; and shall surrender emergency or temporary power when its lawful purpose has ended.
